Distributional Robustness and Transfer Learning Through Empirical Bayes

Michael Law Supported in part by NSF Grant DMS-2203012. Seminar for Statistics, ETH Zürich Peter Bühlmann Supported in part by European Research Council (ERC), European Union’s Horizon 2020 research and innovation programme, grant agreement No. 786461. Seminar for Statistics, ETH Zürich Ya’acov Ritov Supported in part by NSF Grant DMS-2113364. Department of Statistics, University of Michigan
Abstract

We consider the problem of statistical inference on parameters of a target population when auxiliary observations are available from related populations. We propose a flexible empirical Bayes approach that can be applied on top of any asymptotically linear estimator to incorporate information from related populations when constructing confidence regions. The proposed methodology is valid regardless of whether there are direct observations on the population of interest. We demonstrate the performance of the empirical Bayes confidence regions on synthetic data as well as on the Trends in International Mathematics and Sciences Study when using the debiased Lasso as the basic algorithm in high-dimensional regression.

1 Introduction

In classical parametric statistics, we are interested in constructing a confidence interval for a parameter θ=θ​(P)∈Θ𝜃𝜃𝑃Θ\theta=\theta(P)\in\Theta on the basis of n𝑛n independent and identically distributed observations from P𝑃P, which provides a meaningful interpretation for new individuals being drawn from P𝑃P. However, by viewing all individuals as coming from a single large population P𝑃P might in fact be overly simplistic and ignores heterogeneity within our population. For example, if P=Pk𝑃subscript𝑃𝑘P=P_{k} describes the population in environment k𝑘k, we might expect the parameter of interest θ​(Pk)𝜃subscript𝑃𝑘\theta(P_{k}) to vary across our different environments. Due to this heterogeneity amongst our environments, we may no longer be interested in θ​(P)𝜃𝑃\theta(P), the parameter value for the homogenized population, but rather θ​(Pk)𝜃subscript𝑃𝑘\theta(P_{k}), the parameter value for the local population of interest. A canonical example is the one-way random effects model where the global mean is θ​(P)=μ𝜃𝑃𝜇\theta(P)=\mu and the local mean is θ​(Pk)=μ+αk𝜃subscript𝑃𝑘𝜇subscript𝛼𝑘\theta(P_{k})=\mu+\alpha_{k}. Unlike the usual random effect models, we do not view the random component as nuisance, but rather as a source of information that can improve our inference of θ​(Pk)𝜃subscript𝑃𝑘\theta(P_{k}).

To fix some notation, we write P0subscript𝑃0P_{0} to denote the population of interest and P1,…,PKsubscript𝑃1…subscript𝑃𝐾P_{1},\dots,P_{K} to denote the K𝐾K populations that are similar to P0subscript𝑃0P_{0}. Moreover, for k=0,1,…,K𝑘01…𝐾k=0,1,\dots,K, we let nksubscript𝑛𝑘n_{k} denote the number of observations from Pksubscript𝑃𝑘P_{k}. This gives rise to two related, yet distinct, practical problems. In the first setting, we only have observations from P1,…,PKsubscript𝑃1…subscript𝑃𝐾P_{1},\dots,P_{K}, but are interested in a new, typical population P0subscript𝑃0P_{0}. This arises naturally in a cluster sampling framework, where we sample K𝐾K populations but want to generalize to a new population P0subscript𝑃0P_{0}. We refer to this setting as “distributional robustness” since we want an interval that is robust to small distributional perturbations in our parameter of interest. In the second setting, we have observations from P1,…,PKsubscript𝑃1…subscript𝑃𝐾P_{1},\dots,P_{K} and some observations from P0subscript𝑃0P_{0}. We refer to this as the “transfer learning” setting since we try to borrow information from related populations P1,…,PKsubscript𝑃1…subscript𝑃𝐾P_{1},\dots,P_{K} to improve our inference for the target population P0subscript𝑃0P_{0}.

For both settings, we consider a unified framework to construct confidence intervals for θ0≜θ​(P0)≜subscript𝜃0𝜃subscript𝑃0\theta_{0}\triangleq\theta(P_{0}). The idea is to leverage the heterogeneity across our various populations. By viewing θ=θ​(Pk)𝜃𝜃subscript𝑃𝑘\theta=\theta(P_{k}) as a random effect, with the randomness coming from our environments e∈E𝑒𝐸e\in E, this induces a distribution π𝜋\pi on the parameter space ΘΘ\Theta. We emphasize that, although π𝜋\pi is a distribution on the parameter space ΘΘ\Theta, our approach is decidedly non-Bayesian in both a methodological and philosophical perspective. In particular, π𝜋\pi is not a subjective a priori distribution, but rather an objective probability distribution characterizing the variability of θ𝜃\theta induced by our naturally occurring environments. Throughout, we consider for simplicity one-dimensional Euclidean parameters (ie., Θ⊆ℝΘℝ\Theta\subseteq\mathbb{R}), but the theory can be extended to multivariate parameters.

Therefore, we assume the underlying hierarchical model: for every k=0,1,…,K𝑘01…𝐾k=0,1,\dots,K,

Xi,k|θk,ηk∼i​i​df(⋅|θk,ηk),θk∼i​i​d​π​(⋅)\displaystyle\begin{aligned} X_{i,k}|{\theta}_{k},{\eta}_{k}&\overset{iid}{\sim}f(\cdot|{\theta}_{k},{\eta}_{k}),\\ {\theta}_{k}&\overset{iid}{\sim}\pi(\cdot)\end{aligned} (1)

for i=1,…,nk𝑖1…subscript𝑛𝑘i=1,\dots,n_{k}. Here, ηksubscript𝜂𝑘{\eta}_{k} is the nuisance parameter corresponding to population Pksubscript𝑃𝑘P_{k}, which we assume has fixed, deterministic values. We write 𝐗ksubscript𝐗𝑘\mathbf{X}_{k} to denote the data from population Pksubscript𝑃𝑘P_{k} for k=0,…,K𝑘0…𝐾k=0,\dots,K, 𝐗−0subscript𝐗0\mathbf{X}_{-0} to denote the data from P1,…,PKsubscript𝑃1…subscript𝑃𝐾P_{1},\dots,P_{K}, and 𝐗𝐗\mathbf{X} to denote the data from all of the populations. Likewise, 𝜽𝜽\boldsymbol{\theta} denotes the vector of θ0,…,θKsubscript𝜃0…subscript𝜃𝐾\theta_{0},\dots,{\theta}_{K}. Then, following ? (?), the goal is to construct empirical Bayes confidence region.

Definition 1.

For 0<α<10𝛼10<\alpha<1, a region ℐ=ℐ​(𝐗)⊆Θℐℐ𝐗Θ\mathcal{I}=\mathcal{I}(\mathbf{X})\subseteq\Theta is a 1−α1𝛼1-\alpha empirical Bayes confidence region for θ0subscript𝜃0\theta_{0} if

pr𝐗,𝜽⁡(ℐ​(𝐗)∋θ0)≥1−α.subscriptpr𝐗𝜽subscript𝜃0ℐ𝐗1𝛼\displaystyle\operatorname{pr}_{\mathbf{X},\boldsymbol{\theta}}\Big{(}\mathcal{I}(\mathbf{X})\ni\theta_{0}\Big{)}\geq 1-\alpha.
Definition 2.

For 0<α<10𝛼10<\alpha<1, a region ℐ=ℐ​(𝐗)⊆Θℐℐ𝐗Θ\mathcal{I}=\mathcal{I}(\mathbf{X})\subseteq\Theta is a 1−α1𝛼1-\alpha confidence region for θ0subscript𝜃0\theta_{0} if

pr𝐗|𝜽⁡(ℐ​(𝐗)∋θ0)≥1−α.subscriptprconditional𝐗𝜽subscript𝜃0ℐ𝐗1𝛼\displaystyle\operatorname{pr}_{\mathbf{X}|\boldsymbol{\theta}}\Big{(}\mathcal{I}(\mathbf{X})\ni\theta_{0}\Big{)}\geq 1-\alpha.

The primary difference between empirical Bayes confidence regions and classical confidence regions is unconditional versus conditional coverage of θ0subscript𝜃0\theta_{0} over 𝜽𝜽\boldsymbol{\theta}. Empirical Bayes confidence regions do not ensure nominal coverage for each fixed value of θ0subscript𝜃0\theta_{0}, but rather on average over all θ∈Θ𝜃Θ\theta\in\Theta. In the setting of distributional robustness, where we try to predict the value of a random variable, conditional coverage necessarily implies infinitely large confidence regions if ΘΘ\Theta is unbounded. For transfer learning, depending on the particular application, unconditional coverage may be sufficient.

1.1 Existing Works and Our Contributions

Empirical Bayes is one of the earliest tools in statistics introduced by ? (?) to combine information from various populations together. In the context of compound decision theory, empirical Bayes yields estimators that have lower aggregate risk compared to viewing the populations separately. Though initially used mainly for parameter estimation, ? (?) proposed using the tools of empirical Bayes for statistical inference; he focused on the parametric problem and does not provide a general methodological approach. For modern references on empirical Bayes, we refer the interested reader to ? (?) and ? (?) and the references therein.

The work most similar to ours is that of ? (?), who propose a method to construct confidence intervals for 𝔼π​[h​(θ)|𝐗=𝒙]subscript𝔼𝜋delimited-[]conditionalℎ𝜃𝐗𝒙\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[h(\theta)|\mathbf{X}=\boldsymbol{x}] for a known function h​(⋅)ℎ⋅h(\cdot). Whereas they try to capture the conditional expectation of a function of θ𝜃\theta given the data, we target the parameter θ𝜃\theta directly. To help illuminate the difference, consider the special case where n0=0subscript𝑛00n_{0}=0. On one hand, we are interested in predicting the value of θ0=θ​(P0)subscript𝜃0𝜃subscript𝑃0\theta_{0}=\theta(P_{0}) in an unobserved population. On the other hand, ? (?) can only construct a confidence interval for quantities such as 𝔼π​[θ0]subscript𝔼𝜋delimited-[]subscript𝜃0\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[\theta_{0}], the mean in population P0subscript𝑃0P_{0} if h​(⋅)ℎ⋅h(\cdot) is the identity function. This difference in objectives is reflected in the methodological approaches. In the terminology of ? (?), the approach of ? (?) is that of f𝑓f-modeling, whereby the marginal distribution of the data is estimated. Conversely, the approach of the present paper is that of g𝑔g-modeling, whereby the distribution of π​(⋅)𝜋⋅\pi(\cdot) is directly estimated.

1.2 Organization

We end this section with a description of the notation used throughout. Then, in Section 2, we further motivate our methodology by revisiting the Gaussian one-way random effects model. Next, we generalize these ideas to a broader class of models and consider the theoretical performance of an oracle that has access to the true prior in Section 3. In Section 4, we consider general conditions under which the empirical Bayes methodology has asymptotic 1−α1𝛼1-\alpha coverage. Then, we demonstrate the versatility of our results on asymptotically linear estimators in Section 5 and show how to estimate π​(⋅)𝜋⋅\pi(\cdot) with a parametric Gaussian prior and a general nonparametric prior in Subsections 5.1 and 5.2. Finally, in Sections 6 and 7, we analyze the empirical performance of our proposed methodology on synthetic data and to the Trends in Mathematics and Sciences Study, respectively. For ease of presentation, we defer the proof of all results and supplemental lemmata to Section 8.

1.3 Notation

We note that all of our parameters implicitly depend on a parameter n𝑛n; for example, the number of environments is K=K​(n)𝐾𝐾𝑛K=K(n) and the sample sizes are n0=n0​(n),n1=n1​(n),…,nK=nK​(n)formulae-sequencesubscript𝑛0subscript𝑛0𝑛formulae-sequencesubscript𝑛1subscript𝑛1𝑛…subscript𝑛𝐾subscript𝑛𝐾𝑛n_{0}=n_{0}(n),n_{1}=n_{1}(n),\dots,n_{K}=n_{K}(n). For ease of presentation, we omit this dependence on n𝑛n when, by our humble judgement, it should not cause confusion. Moreover, we define θ(n)superscript𝜃𝑛\theta^{(n)} to be the parameter value θ𝜃\theta of an arbitrary population at n𝑛n with an asymptotically linear estimator θ^(n)superscript^𝜃𝑛\hat{\theta}^{(n)}, which simultaneously covers low and high-dimensional settings (see Section 5 for a formal definition). Throughout, we write π​(⋅)=πn​(⋅)𝜋⋅subscript𝜋𝑛⋅\pi(\cdot)=\pi_{n}(\cdot) to both denote the density of our random effects over ΘΘ\Theta; the dependence of π​(⋅)𝜋⋅\pi(\cdot) on n𝑛n allows for contiguity of the prior. We let λ​(⋅)𝜆⋅\lambda(\cdot) denote Lebesgue measure on ℝmsuperscriptℝ𝑚\mathbb{R}^{m} for all m≥1𝑚1m\geq 1, with the dimension being implicit. We write φ(⋅|μ,σ2)=φμ,σ2(⋅)\varphi(\cdot|\mu,\sigma^{2})=\varphi_{\mu,\sigma^{2}}(\cdot) and Φ(⋅|μ,σ2)=Φμ,σ2(⋅)\Phi(\cdot|\mu,\sigma^{2})=\Phi_{\mu,\sigma^{2}}(\cdot) to denote the density and distribution function of a Gaussian random variable with mean μ𝜇\mu and variance σ2superscript𝜎2\sigma^{2}.

2 Gaussian One-Way Random Effects Model

As a motivating example, consider the simple one-way Gaussian random effects model with covariates:

yi,k=θk+⟨𝒙i,k,𝜷k⟩2+εi,ksubscript𝑦𝑖𝑘subscript𝜃𝑘subscriptsubscript𝒙𝑖𝑘subscript𝜷𝑘2subscript𝜀𝑖𝑘\displaystyle y_{i,k}={\theta}_{k}+\langle\boldsymbol{x}_{i,k},\boldsymbol{\beta}_{k}\rangle_{2}+\varepsilon_{i,k}

for k=0,…,K𝑘0…𝐾k=0,\dots,K and i=1,…,nk𝑖1…subscript𝑛𝑘i=1,\dots,n_{k}. For simplicity, we assume that π𝜋\pi is Gaussian with mean μ𝜇\mu and variance σπ2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2\sigma_{\pi}^{2} that are known and σε2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2} is also known. There are a few ways of viewing this as a distributional robustness or transfer learning problem:

  1. 1.

    If we have no way to connect the K𝐾K groups, the best we can do is a linear regression in population P0subscript𝑃0P_{0}.

  2. 2.

    If 𝜷=𝜷0=⋯=𝜷K𝜷subscript𝜷0⋯subscript𝜷𝐾\boldsymbol{\beta}=\boldsymbol{\beta}_{0}=\dots=\boldsymbol{\beta}_{K} and we do not know the prior π𝜋\pi or K𝐾K is small, then we can estimate the regression coefficients on the other populations and return this to the adaptive setting.

  3. 3.

    If 𝜷0≠𝜷ksubscript𝜷0subscript𝜷𝑘\boldsymbol{\beta}_{0}\neq\boldsymbol{\beta}_{k} for k=1,…,K𝑘1…𝐾k=1,\dots,K but we know the prior π𝜋\pi, then our only advantage is to use the prior distribution and then consider the posterior. For simplicity, we only consider a Gaussian π𝜋\pi with mean μ𝜇\mu and variance σπ2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2\sigma_{\pi}^{2}, corresponding to the usual Gaussian random effects model.

  4. 4.

    If 𝜷=𝜷0=⋯=𝜷K𝜷subscript𝜷0⋯subscript𝜷𝐾\boldsymbol{\beta}=\boldsymbol{\beta}_{0}=\dots=\boldsymbol{\beta}_{K} and we know the prior π𝜋\pi, then we can combine the two above approaches.

Below, we compute explicitly the variances of the resultant estimators, which implies the width of the resultant interval.

  1. 1.

    In this case, we should use least-squares to estimate θ0subscript𝜃0\theta_{0}, which is known to be parametrically efficient. Letting 𝐗0∈ℝn0×psubscript𝐗0superscriptℝsubscript𝑛0𝑝\mathbf{X}_{0}\in\mathbb{R}^{n_{0}\times p} denote the design matrix in population P0subscript𝑃0P_{0}, we have

    θ^0|θ0∼𝒩​(θ0,σε2n0−𝟏n0⊤​𝐗0​(𝐗0⊤​𝐗0)−1​𝐗0⊤​𝟏n0).similar-toconditionalsubscript^𝜃0subscript𝜃0𝒩subscript𝜃0superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript1subscript𝑛0topsubscript𝐗0superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript𝐗01superscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript1subscript𝑛0\displaystyle\hat{\theta}_{0}|\theta_{0}\sim\mathcal{N}\Big{(}\theta_{0},\frac{\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}}{n_{0}-\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0}(\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0})^{-1}\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}}\Big{)}.
  2. 2.

    If 𝜷𝜷\boldsymbol{\beta} is common, we can estimate all of the parameters jointly on the pooled data. For simplicity, suppose K=1𝐾1K=1. Then, we have

    (𝒚0𝒚1)=(𝟏n0𝟎n0𝐗0𝟎n1𝟏n1𝐗1)​(θ0θ1𝜷)+(𝜺0𝜺1),matrixsubscript𝒚0subscript𝒚1matrixsubscript1subscript𝑛0subscript0subscript𝑛0subscript𝐗0subscript0subscript𝑛1subscript1subscript𝑛1subscript𝐗1matrixsubscript𝜃0subscript𝜃1𝜷matrixsubscript𝜺0subscript𝜺1\displaystyle\begin{pmatrix}\boldsymbol{y}_{0}\\ \boldsymbol{y}_{1}\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}&\mathbf{0}_{n_{0}}&\mathbf{X}_{0}\\ \mathbf{0}_{n_{1}}&\mathbf{1}_{n_{1}}&\mathbf{X}_{1}\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}\theta_{0}\\ \theta_{1}\\ \boldsymbol{\beta}\end{pmatrix}+\begin{pmatrix}\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{0}\\ \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{1}\end{pmatrix},

    and, hence,

    θ^0|θ0∼𝒩​(θ0,σε2n0−𝟏n0⊤​𝐗0​(𝐗0⊤​𝐗0+𝐗1⊤​𝐗1)−1​𝐗0⊤​𝟏n0).similar-toconditionalsubscript^𝜃0subscript𝜃0𝒩subscript𝜃0superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript1subscript𝑛0topsubscript𝐗0superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript𝐗0superscriptsubscript𝐗1topsubscript𝐗11superscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript1subscript𝑛0\displaystyle\hat{\theta}_{0}|\theta_{0}\sim\mathcal{N}\Big{(}\theta_{0},\frac{\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}}{n_{0}-\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0}(\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0}+\mathbf{X}_{1}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{1})^{-1}\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}}\Big{)}.

    More generally, if K>1𝐾1K>1, we have

    θ^0|θ0∼𝒩​(θ0,σε2n0−𝟏n0⊤​𝐗0​(∑k=0K𝐗k⊤​𝐗k)−1​𝐗0⊤​𝟏n0).similar-toconditionalsubscript^𝜃0subscript𝜃0𝒩subscript𝜃0superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript1subscript𝑛0topsubscript𝐗0superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑘0𝐾superscriptsubscript𝐗𝑘topsubscript𝐗𝑘1superscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript1subscript𝑛0\displaystyle\hat{\theta}_{0}|\theta_{0}\sim\mathcal{N}\Big{(}\theta_{0},\frac{\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}}{n_{0}-\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0}(\sum_{k=0}^{K}\mathbf{X}_{k}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{k})^{-1}\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}}\Big{)}.

    Compared to the first case, the variance in the second case is always smaller than or equal to the variance in the first case. To see this, note that, by the matrix inversion lemma,

    (∑k=0K𝐗k⊤​𝐗k)−1=(𝐗0⊤​𝐗0)−1−(𝐗0⊤​𝐗0)−1​[(∑k=1K𝐗k⊤​𝐗k)−1+(𝐗0⊤​𝐗0)−1]−1​(𝐗0⊤​𝐗0)−1.superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑘0𝐾superscriptsubscript𝐗𝑘topsubscript𝐗𝑘1superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript𝐗01superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript𝐗01superscriptdelimited-[]superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑘1𝐾superscriptsubscript𝐗𝑘topsubscript𝐗𝑘1superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript𝐗011superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript𝐗01\displaystyle\Big{(}\sum_{k=0}^{K}\mathbf{X}_{k}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{k}\Big{)}^{-1}=(\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0})^{-1}-(\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0})^{-1}\Big{[}\Big{(}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\mathbf{X}_{k}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{k}\Big{)}^{-1}+(\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0})^{-1}\Big{]}^{-1}(\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0})^{-1}.

    Hence, the difference in the two denominators of the variance term is

    {n0−𝟏n0⊤​𝐗0​(𝐗0⊤​𝐗0)−1​𝐗0⊤​𝟏n0}−{n0−𝟏n0⊤​𝐗0​(∑k=0K𝐗k⊤​𝐗k)−1​𝐗0⊤​𝟏n0}subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript1subscript𝑛0topsubscript𝐗0superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript𝐗01superscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript1subscript𝑛0subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript1subscript𝑛0topsubscript𝐗0superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑘0𝐾superscriptsubscript𝐗𝑘topsubscript𝐗𝑘1superscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript1subscript𝑛0\displaystyle\Big{\{}n_{0}-\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0}(\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0})^{-1}\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}\Big{\}}-\Big{\{}n_{0}-\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0}\Big{(}\sum_{k=0}^{K}\mathbf{X}_{k}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{k}\Big{)}^{-1}\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}\Big{\}}
    =𝟏n0⊤​(𝐗0⊤​𝐗0)−1​[(∑k=1K𝐗k⊤​𝐗k)−1+(𝐗0⊤​𝐗0)−1]−1​(𝐗0⊤​𝐗0)−1​𝐗0⊤​𝟏n0absentsuperscriptsubscript1subscript𝑛0topsuperscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript𝐗01superscriptdelimited-[]superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑘1𝐾superscriptsubscript𝐗𝑘topsubscript𝐗𝑘1superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript𝐗011superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript𝐗01superscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript1subscript𝑛0\displaystyle\phantom{\leq}=\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}^{\top}(\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0})^{-1}\Big{[}\Big{(}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\mathbf{X}_{k}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{k}\Big{)}^{-1}+(\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0})^{-1}\Big{]}^{-1}(\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0})^{-1}\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}
    ≥0.absent0\displaystyle\phantom{\leq}\geq 0.

    The last inequality follows from the positive semidefiniteness of the middle term.

  3. 3.

    If π𝜋\pi is Gaussian but the 𝜷𝜷\boldsymbol{\beta} are different, then we may estimate θ^0subscript^𝜃0\hat{\theta}_{0} and then consider the posterior distribution θ0|θ^0conditionalsubscript𝜃0subscript^𝜃0\theta_{0}|\hat{\theta}_{0}. Now, it follows from the first case that the posterior distribution is given by

    θ0|θ^0∼𝒩(\displaystyle\theta_{0}|\hat{\theta}_{0}\sim\mathcal{N}\Big{(} σε2σπ2​{n0−𝟏n0⊤​𝐗0​(𝐗0⊤​𝐗0)−1​𝐗0⊤​𝟏n0}+σε2​μ+σπ2​{n0−𝟏n0⊤​𝐗0​(𝐗0⊤​𝐗0)−1​𝐗0⊤​𝟏n0}σπ2​{n0−𝟏n0⊤​𝐗0​(𝐗0⊤​𝐗0)−1​𝐗0⊤​𝟏n0}+σε2​θ^0,superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript1subscript𝑛0topsubscript𝐗0superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript𝐗01superscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript1subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2𝜇superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript1subscript𝑛0topsubscript𝐗0superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript𝐗01superscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript1subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript1subscript𝑛0topsubscript𝐗0superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript𝐗01superscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript1subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2subscript^𝜃0\displaystyle\frac{\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}}{\sigma_{\pi}^{2}\{n_{0}-\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0}(\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0})^{-1}\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}\}+\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}}\mu+\frac{\sigma_{\pi}^{2}\{n_{0}-\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0}(\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0})^{-1}\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}\}}{\sigma_{\pi}^{2}\{n_{0}-\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0}(\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0})^{-1}\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}\}+\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}}\hat{\theta}_{0},
    σπ2​σε2σπ2​{n0−𝟏n0⊤​𝐗0​(𝐗0⊤​𝐗0)−1​𝐗0⊤​𝟏n0}+σε2).\displaystyle\phantom{\leq}\frac{\sigma_{\pi}^{2}\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}}{\sigma_{\pi}^{2}\{n_{0}-\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0}(\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0})^{-1}\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}\}+\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}}\Big{)}.

    In the usual one-way Gaussian random effects model, we assume μ=0𝜇0\mu=0, and so the above reduces to

    θ0|θ^0∼𝒩​(σπ2​{n0−𝟏n0⊤​𝐗0​(𝐗0⊤​𝐗0)−1​𝐗0⊤​𝟏n0}σπ2​{n0−𝟏n0⊤​𝐗0​(𝐗0⊤​𝐗0)−1​𝐗0⊤​𝟏n0}+σε2​θ^0,σπ2​σε2σπ2​{n0−𝟏n0⊤​𝐗0​(𝐗0⊤​𝐗0)−1​𝐗0⊤​𝟏n0}+σε2).similar-toconditionalsubscript𝜃0subscript^𝜃0𝒩superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript1subscript𝑛0topsubscript𝐗0superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript𝐗01superscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript1subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript1subscript𝑛0topsubscript𝐗0superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript𝐗01superscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript1subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2subscript^𝜃0superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript1subscript𝑛0topsubscript𝐗0superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript𝐗01superscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript1subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2\displaystyle\theta_{0}|\hat{\theta}_{0}\sim\mathcal{N}\Big{(}\frac{\sigma_{\pi}^{2}\{n_{0}-\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0}(\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0})^{-1}\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}\}}{\sigma_{\pi}^{2}\{n_{0}-\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0}(\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0})^{-1}\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}\}+\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}}\hat{\theta}_{0},\frac{\sigma_{\pi}^{2}\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}}{\sigma_{\pi}^{2}\{n_{0}-\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0}(\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0})^{-1}\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}\}+\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}}\Big{)}.

    Note that the variance of the posterior is always smaller than the variance in the first case since

    σε2n0−𝟏n0⊤​𝐗0​(𝐗0⊤​𝐗0)−1​𝐗0⊤​𝟏n0−σπ2​σε2σπ2​{n0−𝟏n0⊤​𝐗0​(𝐗0⊤​𝐗0)−1​𝐗0⊤​𝟏n0}+σε2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript1subscript𝑛0topsubscript𝐗0superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript𝐗01superscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript1subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript1subscript𝑛0topsubscript𝐗0superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript𝐗01superscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript1subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2\displaystyle\frac{\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}}{n_{0}-\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0}(\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0})^{-1}\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}}-\frac{\sigma_{\pi}^{2}\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}}{\sigma_{\pi}^{2}\{n_{0}-\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0}(\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0})^{-1}\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}\}+\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}}
    =σε2n0−𝟏n0⊤​𝐗0​(𝐗0⊤​𝐗0)−1​𝐗0⊤​𝟏n0​(1−σπ2​{n0−𝟏n0⊤​𝐗0​(𝐗0⊤​𝐗0)−1​𝐗0⊤​𝟏n0}σπ2​{n0−𝟏n0⊤​𝐗0​(𝐗0⊤​𝐗0)−1​𝐗0⊤​𝟏n0}+σε2)absentsuperscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript1subscript𝑛0topsubscript𝐗0superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript𝐗01superscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript1subscript𝑛01superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript1subscript𝑛0topsubscript𝐗0superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript𝐗01superscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript1subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript1subscript𝑛0topsubscript𝐗0superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript𝐗01superscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript1subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2\displaystyle\phantom{\leq}=\frac{\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}}{n_{0}-\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0}(\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0})^{-1}\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}}\Big{(}1-\frac{\sigma_{\pi}^{2}\{n_{0}-\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0}(\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0})^{-1}\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}\}}{\sigma_{\pi}^{2}\{n_{0}-\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0}(\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0})^{-1}\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}\}+\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}}\Big{)}
    >0.absent0\displaystyle\phantom{\leq}>0.

    However, depending on the nature of the design matrices, the variance could be smaller than, equal to, or greater than the second case. One advantage of this formulation is in the setting where n0=0subscript𝑛00n_{0}=0. In this case, the above reduces to

    θ0∼𝒩​(0,σπ2),similar-tosubscript𝜃0𝒩0superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2\displaystyle\theta_{0}\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_{\pi}^{2}),

    and so we may still obtain a bounded interval yielding the correct coverage.

  4. 4.

    When 𝜷𝜷\boldsymbol{\beta} is shared across all groups and π𝜋\pi is Gaussian, we may combine the approaches from the second and third case. Assuming μ=0𝜇0\mu=0, then the posterior distribution of θ0subscript𝜃0\theta_{0}

    θ0|θ^0∼𝒩(\displaystyle\theta_{0}|\hat{\theta}_{0}\sim\mathcal{N}\Big{(} σπ2​{n0−𝟏n0⊤​𝐗0​(∑k=0K𝐗k⊤​𝐗k)−1​𝐗0⊤​𝟏n0}σπ2​{n0−𝟏n0⊤​𝐗0​(∑k=0K𝐗k⊤​𝐗k)−1​𝐗0⊤​𝟏n0}+σε2​θ^0,superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript1subscript𝑛0topsubscript𝐗0superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑘0𝐾superscriptsubscript𝐗𝑘topsubscript𝐗𝑘1superscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript1subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript1subscript𝑛0topsubscript𝐗0superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑘0𝐾superscriptsubscript𝐗𝑘topsubscript𝐗𝑘1superscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript1subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2subscript^𝜃0\displaystyle\frac{\sigma_{\pi}^{2}\{n_{0}-\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0}(\sum_{k=0}^{K}\mathbf{X}_{k}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{k})^{-1}\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}\}}{\sigma_{\pi}^{2}\{n_{0}-\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0}(\sum_{k=0}^{K}\mathbf{X}_{k}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{k})^{-1}\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}\}+\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}}\hat{\theta}_{0},
    σπ2​σε2σπ2​{n0−𝟏n0⊤​𝐗0​(∑k=0K𝐗k⊤​𝐗k)−1​𝐗0⊤​𝟏n0}+σε2).\displaystyle\phantom{\leq}\frac{\sigma_{\pi}^{2}\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}}{\sigma_{\pi}^{2}\{n_{0}-\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{0}(\sum_{k=0}^{K}\mathbf{X}_{k}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{k})^{-1}\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}\}+\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}}\Big{)}.

    By the above calculations, it is immediate that this interval yields the shortest width of the ones considered.

3 Oracle Bayes

In this section, we generalize the ideas in Section 2 and consider an arbitrary statistic 𝐔0=𝐔0​(𝐗0)∈ℝu0subscript𝐔0subscript𝐔0subscript𝐗0superscriptℝsubscript𝑢0\mathbf{U}_{0}=\mathbf{U}_{0}(\mathbf{X}_{0})\in\mathbb{R}^{u_{0}}. For example, 𝐔0subscript𝐔0\mathbf{U}_{0} may be the full data 𝐗0subscript𝐗0\mathbf{X}_{0}, an estimator θ^0subscript^𝜃0\hat{\theta}_{0} of θ0subscript𝜃0\theta_{0}, or simply nothing in the case of distributional robustness (n0=u0=0subscript𝑛0subscript𝑢00n_{0}=u_{0}=0). To motivate our general approach, we consider an oracle that has access to π​(⋅)𝜋⋅\pi(\cdot) and the density of 𝐔0subscript𝐔0\mathbf{U}_{0}, which we denote by f​(𝐔0|θ0)𝑓conditionalsubscript𝐔0subscript𝜃0f(\mathbf{U}_{0}|\theta_{0}). The oracle may directly consider the posterior distribution of θ0|𝐔0conditionalsubscript𝜃0subscript𝐔0\theta_{0}|\mathbf{U}_{0}; in particular, letting π(⋅|𝐔0)\pi(\cdot|\mathbf{U}_{0}) denote the posterior density, we have

π​(θ0|𝐔0)=f​(𝐔0|θ0)​π​(θ0)f​(𝐔0),𝜋conditionalsubscript𝜃0subscript𝐔0𝑓conditionalsubscript𝐔0subscript𝜃0𝜋subscript𝜃0𝑓subscript𝐔0\displaystyle\pi(\theta_{0}|\mathbf{U}_{0})=\frac{f(\mathbf{U}_{0}|\theta_{0})\pi(\theta_{0})}{f(\mathbf{U}_{0})},

where

f​(𝐔0)=∫Θf​(𝐔0|θ0)​π​(θ0)​λ​(d​θ0)𝑓subscript𝐔0subscriptΘ𝑓conditionalsubscript𝐔0subscript𝜃0𝜋subscript𝜃0𝜆𝑑subscript𝜃0\displaystyle f(\mathbf{U}_{0})=\int_{\Theta}f(\mathbf{U}_{0}|\theta_{0})\pi(\theta_{0})\lambda(d\theta_{0})

is the marginal density of 𝐔0subscript𝐔0\mathbf{U}_{0}. Then, for some threshold τ=τn​(α)>0𝜏subscript𝜏𝑛𝛼0\tau=\tau_{n}(\alpha)>0, we define the oracle Bayes confidence region as

ℐob≜{θ∈Θ:π​(θ|𝐔0)>τ}.≜subscriptℐobconditional-set𝜃Θ𝜋conditional𝜃subscript𝐔0𝜏\displaystyle\mathcal{I}_{\text{ob}}\triangleq\Big{\{}\theta\in\Theta:\pi(\theta|\mathbf{U}_{0})>\tau\Big{\}}.

In the special case of distributional robustness, the posterior density is equal to the prior density and the oracle Bayes confidence region is therefore

ℐob={θ∈Θ:π(θ)>τ)}.\displaystyle\mathcal{I}_{\text{ob}}=\Big{\{}\theta\in\Theta:\pi(\theta)>\tau)\Big{\}}.

In both cases, the value of τ𝜏\tau is defined through

τ𝜏\displaystyle\tau ≜arg​maxτ>0⁡{pr𝐔0,θ0⁡(ℐob∋θ0)≥1−α}≜absentsubscriptargmax𝜏0subscriptprsubscript𝐔0subscript𝜃0subscript𝜃0subscriptℐob1𝛼\displaystyle\triangleq\operatorname*{arg\,max}_{\tau>0}\Big{\{}\operatorname{pr}_{\mathbf{U}_{0},\theta_{0}}(\mathcal{I}_{\text{ob}}\ni\theta_{0})\geq 1-\alpha\Big{\}}
=arg​maxτ>0⁡{∫ℝu0f​(𝐔0)​λ​(d​𝐔0)​∫Θ𝟙​{ϑ∈Θ:π​(ϑ|𝐔0)>τ}​π​(θ|𝐔0)​λ​(d​θ)≥1−α}.absentsubscriptargmax𝜏0subscriptsuperscriptℝsubscript𝑢0𝑓subscript𝐔0𝜆𝑑subscript𝐔0subscriptΘ1conditional-setitalic-ϑΘ𝜋conditionalitalic-ϑsubscript𝐔0𝜏𝜋conditional𝜃subscript𝐔0𝜆𝑑𝜃1𝛼\displaystyle=\operatorname*{arg\,max}_{\tau>0}\Big{\{}\int_{\mathbb{R}^{u_{0}}}f(\mathbf{U}_{0})\lambda(d\mathbf{U}_{0})\int_{\Theta}\mathbbm{1}\left\{\vartheta\in\Theta:\pi(\vartheta|\mathbf{U}_{0})>\tau\right\}\pi(\theta|\mathbf{U}_{0})\lambda(d\theta)\geq 1-\alpha\Big{\}}.

By construction, it follows that ℐobsubscriptℐob\mathcal{I}_{\text{ob}} attains the nominal 1−α1𝛼1-\alpha coverage. Note that ℐobsubscriptℐob\mathcal{I}_{\text{ob}} is almost mathematically equivalent to the posterior Bayesian highest density region for θ0subscript𝜃0\theta_{0} given the data 𝐔0subscript𝐔0\mathbf{U}_{0}. Compared to the subjective Bayesian credible region, the critical threshold τ𝜏\tau here also integrates over the marginal distribution of 𝐔0subscript𝐔0\mathbf{U}_{0}, whereas the Bayesian region simply considers the inner integral; more accurately, ℐobsubscriptℐob\mathcal{I}_{\text{ob}} is an oracle marginal Bayesian confidence region, though we omit this distinction in subsequent discussions. Moreover, we interpret ℐobsubscriptℐob\mathcal{I}_{\text{ob}} as the objective oracle Bayes confidence region for θ0subscript𝜃0\theta_{0} as, at the population level over the randomness of our environments, θ0subscript𝜃0\theta_{0} is an independent random variable with random effects distribution π​(⋅)𝜋⋅\pi(\cdot). Though the interpretation between ℐobsubscriptℐob\mathcal{I}_{\text{ob}} and Bayesian credible regions are philosophically different, the Bayesian formalism immediately implies desirable theoretical properties for ℐobsubscriptℐob\mathcal{I}_{\text{ob}} as the following proposition demonstrates.

Proposition 1.

Let ℐ=ℐ​(𝐔0)⊆Θℐℐsubscript𝐔0Θ\mathcal{I}=\mathcal{I}(\mathbf{U}_{0})\subseteq\Theta be an arbitrary random set satisfying

pr𝐔0,θ0⁡(ℐ∋θ0)≥1−α.subscriptprsubscript𝐔0subscript𝜃0subscript𝜃0ℐ1𝛼\displaystyle\operatorname{pr}_{\mathbf{U}_{0},\theta_{0}}(\mathcal{I}\ni\theta_{0})\geq 1-\alpha.

If

pr𝐔0,θ0⁡(ℐob∋θ0)=1−α,subscriptprsubscript𝐔0subscript𝜃0subscript𝜃0subscriptℐob1𝛼\displaystyle\operatorname{pr}_{\mathbf{U}_{0},\theta_{0}}(\mathcal{I}_{\text{ob}}\ni\theta_{0})=1-\alpha,

then

𝔼𝐔0,θ0​λ​(ℐob)≤𝔼𝐔0,θ0​λ​(ℐ).subscript𝔼subscript𝐔0subscript𝜃0𝜆subscriptℐobsubscript𝔼subscript𝐔0subscript𝜃0𝜆ℐ\displaystyle\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{U}_{0},\theta_{0}}\lambda(\mathcal{I}_{\text{ob}})\leq\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{U}_{0},\theta_{0}}\lambda(\mathcal{I}).

Proposition 1 asserts that the oracle Bayes confidence region has the smallest expected Lebesgue measure amongst all random sets covering θ0subscript𝜃0\theta_{0} with probability at least 1−α1𝛼1-\alpha over the joint distribution of 𝐔0subscript𝐔0\mathbf{U}_{0} and θ0subscript𝜃0\theta_{0}. The assumption that the oracle Bayes region has exact 1−α1𝛼1-\alpha coverage is a technical requirement; it is a consequence of the mapping α↦τ​(α)maps-to𝛼𝜏𝛼\alpha\mapsto\tau(\alpha) not being necessarily injective. As an example, if π​(⋅)𝜋⋅\pi(\cdot) is the uniform density on (0,1)01(0,1) and u0=0subscript𝑢00u_{0}=0, then τ​(α)=1𝜏𝛼1\tau(\alpha)=1 for all α∈(0,1)𝛼01\alpha\in(0,1). This is analogous to the non-randomized likelihood ratio test not being the uniformly most powerful test in a simple vs simple hypotheses testing problem when size cannot be attained without randomization. By introducing additional, external randomization, the oracle Bayes confidence region defined above may be modified such that the coverage is exactly 1−α1𝛼1-\alpha and, hence, have the smallest expected Lebesgue measure.

Remark 1.

If 𝐔0subscript𝐔0\mathbf{U}_{0} is a sufficient statistic for θ0subscript𝜃0\theta_{0}, then the conclusion may be strengthened to the oracle Bayes confidence region has the smallest expected Lebesgue measure amongst all random sets covering θ0subscript𝜃0\theta_{0} with probability at least 1−α1𝛼1-\alpha over the joint distribution of 𝐗0subscript𝐗0\mathbf{X}_{0} and θ0subscript𝜃0\theta_{0}, which follows immediately from the factorization theorem. Thus, by conditioning on the full data 𝐗0subscript𝐗0\mathbf{X}_{0}, we have the shortest possible expected Lebesgue measure.

4 Empirical Bayes

In the previous section, we considered the performance of the oracle Bayes confidence region when the prior π​(⋅)𝜋⋅\pi(\cdot) and the density f(⋅|θ0)f(\cdot|\theta_{0}) are known exactly. However, such oracles are not particularly abundant and, in practice, we need to estimate the prior distribution π𝜋\pi. To this end, let π^n​(⋅)subscript^𝜋𝑛⋅\hat{\pi}_{n}(\cdot) be an estimator of πn​(⋅)subscript𝜋𝑛⋅\pi_{n}(\cdot) and f^n(⋅|θ)\hat{f}_{n}(\cdot|\theta) be an estimator of fn(⋅|θ0)f_{n}(\cdot|\theta_{0}), the conditional density of 𝐔0|θ0conditionalsubscript𝐔0subscript𝜃0\mathbf{U}_{0}|\theta_{0}. Here, we include the dependence on n𝑛n to emphasize that the subsequent limits are taken with respect to n𝑛n and to allow for contiguity. Then, the estimated posterior distribution of θ0|𝐔0conditionalsubscript𝜃0subscript𝐔0\theta_{0}|\mathbf{U}_{0} is given by

π^n​(θ0|𝐔0)=fn​(𝐔0|θ0)​π^n​(θ0)f^n​(𝐔0),subscript^𝜋𝑛conditionalsubscript𝜃0subscript𝐔0subscript𝑓𝑛conditionalsubscript𝐔0subscript𝜃0subscript^𝜋𝑛subscript𝜃0subscript^𝑓𝑛subscript𝐔0\displaystyle\hat{\pi}_{n}(\theta_{0}|\mathbf{U}_{0})=\frac{f_{n}(\mathbf{U}_{0}|\theta_{0})\hat{\pi}_{n}(\theta_{0})}{\hat{f}_{n}(\mathbf{U}_{0})},

where

f^n​(𝐔0)=∫Θfn​(𝐔0|θ0)​π^n​(θ0)​λ​(d​θ0)subscript^𝑓𝑛subscript𝐔0subscriptΘsubscript𝑓𝑛conditionalsubscript𝐔0subscript𝜃0subscript^𝜋𝑛subscript𝜃0𝜆𝑑subscript𝜃0\displaystyle\hat{f}_{n}(\mathbf{U}_{0})=\int_{\Theta}f_{n}(\mathbf{U}_{0}|\theta_{0})\hat{\pi}_{n}(\theta_{0})\lambda(d\theta_{0})

is the estimated marginal density of 𝐔0subscript𝐔0\mathbf{U}_{0}. From here, the natural analogue of the oracle Bayes confidence region is given by the following empirical Bayes confidence region:

ℐeb≜{θ∈Θ:π^​(θ|𝐔0)>τ^},≜subscriptℐebconditional-set𝜃Θ^𝜋conditional𝜃subscript𝐔0^𝜏\displaystyle\mathcal{I}_{\text{eb}}\triangleq\Big{\{}\theta\in\Theta:\hat{\pi}(\theta|\mathbf{U}_{0})>\hat{\tau}\Big{\}}, (2)

where

τ^=τ^n​(α)=arg​maxτ>0⁡{∫ℝu0f^n​(𝐔0)​λ​(d​𝐔0)​∫Θ𝟙​{ϑ∈Θ:π^n​(ϑ|𝐔0)>τ}​π^n​(θ|𝐔0)​λ​(d​θ)≥1−α}.^𝜏subscript^𝜏𝑛𝛼subscriptargmax𝜏0subscriptsuperscriptℝsubscript𝑢0subscript^𝑓𝑛subscript𝐔0𝜆𝑑subscript𝐔0subscriptΘ1conditional-setitalic-ϑΘsubscript^𝜋𝑛conditionalitalic-ϑsubscript𝐔0𝜏subscript^𝜋𝑛conditional𝜃subscript𝐔0𝜆𝑑𝜃1𝛼\displaystyle\hat{\tau}=\hat{\tau}_{n}(\alpha)=\operatorname*{arg\,max}_{\tau>0}\Big{\{}\int_{\mathbb{R}^{u_{0}}}\hat{f}_{n}(\mathbf{U}_{0})\lambda(d\mathbf{U}_{0})\int_{\Theta}\mathbbm{1}\left\{\vartheta\in\Theta:\hat{\pi}_{n}(\vartheta|\mathbf{U}_{0})>\tau\right\}\hat{\pi}_{n}(\theta|\mathbf{U}_{0})\lambda(d\theta)\geq 1-\alpha\Big{\}}. (3)

To analyze the performance of ℐebsubscriptℐeb\mathcal{I}_{\text{eb}}, we impose the following mild assumption.

Assumption A.

There exist estimators π^n​(⋅)subscript^𝜋𝑛⋅\hat{\pi}_{n}(\cdot) of πn​(⋅)subscript𝜋𝑛⋅\pi_{n}(\cdot) and f^n​(⋅)subscript^𝑓𝑛⋅\hat{f}_{n}(\cdot) of fn​(⋅)subscript𝑓𝑛⋅f_{n}(\cdot) such that

‖π^n​(⋅)−πn​(⋅)‖1=opr​(1)subscriptnormsubscript^𝜋𝑛⋅subscript𝜋𝑛⋅1subscript𝑜pr1\displaystyle\|\hat{\pi}_{n}(\cdot)-\pi_{n}(\cdot)\|_{1}=o_{\operatorname{pr}}(1)

and

supθ0∈Υ∥f^n(⋅|θ0)−fn(⋅|θ0)∥1=opr(1)\displaystyle\sup_{\theta_{0}\in\Upsilon}\|\hat{f}_{n}(\cdot|\theta_{0})-f_{n}(\cdot|\theta_{0})\|_{1}=o_{\operatorname{pr}}(1)

for all compacts Υ⊆ΘΥΘ\Upsilon\subseteq\Theta. Moreover, πnsubscript𝜋𝑛\pi_{n} converges in total variation distance to a distribution π∞subscript𝜋\pi_{\infty}.

Assumption A requires consistent estimators of both πn​(⋅)subscript𝜋𝑛⋅\pi_{n}(\cdot) and fn(⋅|θ)f_{n}(\cdot|\theta) in the L1superscript𝐿1L^{1} norm; we defer a more detailed discussion on how to estimate fn(⋅|θ)f_{n}(\cdot|\theta) to Section 5 and πn​(⋅)subscript𝜋𝑛⋅\pi_{n}(\cdot) to Subsections 5.1 and 5.2 below. Under the above assumption, we have the following proposition regarding the empirical Bayes confidence region.

Proposition 2.

Suppose Assumption A holds. Then,

∫Θ×ℝu0|fn(𝐔0)πn(θ0|𝐔0)−f^n(𝐔0)π^n(θ0|𝐔0)|λ(dθ0×d𝐔0)=opr(1).\displaystyle\int_{\Theta\times\mathbb{R}^{u_{0}}}\Big{|}f_{n}(\mathbf{U}_{0})\pi_{n}(\theta_{0}|\mathbf{U}_{0})-\hat{f}_{n}(\mathbf{U}_{0})\hat{\pi}_{n}(\theta_{0}|\mathbf{U}_{0})\Big{|}\lambda(d\theta_{0}\times d\mathbf{U}_{0})=o_{\operatorname{pr}}(1).

Proposition 2 asserts that the estimated joint density of (𝐔0,θ0)subscript𝐔0subscript𝜃0(\mathbf{U}_{0},\theta_{0}) converges to the population joint density. By Scheffé’s Lemma, this implies that empirical and population measures converge in total variation distance, which immediately yields the following corollary regarding the asymptotic coverage.

Corollary 2.1.

Suppose Assumption A holds. Then,

lim infn→∞pr𝐔0,θ0⁡(ℐeb∋θ0)≥1−α.subscriptlimit-infimum→𝑛subscriptprsubscript𝐔0subscript𝜃0subscript𝜃0subscriptℐeb1𝛼\displaystyle\liminf_{n\to\infty}\operatorname{pr}_{\mathbf{U}_{0},\theta_{0}}(\mathcal{I}_{\text{eb}}\ni\theta_{0})\geq 1-\alpha.

From here, we immediately have that the empirical Bayes confidence region has asymptotic 1−α1𝛼1-\alpha coverage as long as π^n​(⋅)subscript^𝜋𝑛⋅\hat{\pi}_{n}(\cdot) and f^n(⋅|θ0)\hat{f}_{n}(\cdot|\theta_{0}) converge in total variation. The following example revisits the Gaussian one-way random effects model when 𝜷0subscript𝜷0\boldsymbol{\beta}_{0}, σπ2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2\sigma_{\pi}^{2}, and σε2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2} need to be estimated from the data.

Example 1.

Consider setting 3 from Section 2. That is, we have observations from the one-way Gaussian random effects model

yi,k=θk+⟨𝒙i,k,𝜷k⟩2+εi,ksubscript𝑦𝑖𝑘subscript𝜃𝑘subscriptsubscript𝒙𝑖𝑘subscript𝜷𝑘2subscript𝜀𝑖𝑘\displaystyle y_{i,k}={\theta}_{k}+\langle\boldsymbol{x}_{i,k},\boldsymbol{\beta}_{k}\rangle_{2}+\varepsilon_{i,k}

for k=0,…,K𝑘0…𝐾k=0,\dots,K and i=1,…,nk𝑖1…subscript𝑛𝑘i=1,\dots,n_{k}. Here, θk​∼i​i​d​𝒩​(0,σπ2)subscript𝜃𝑘𝑖𝑖𝑑similar-to𝒩0superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2{\theta}_{k}\overset{iid}{\sim}\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_{\pi}^{2}) and εi,k​∼i​i​d​𝒩​(0,σε2)subscript𝜀𝑖𝑘𝑖𝑖𝑑similar-to𝒩0superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2\varepsilon_{i,k}\overset{iid}{\sim}\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}). In matrix notation, we write

𝒚k=𝐗k​𝜷k+θk​𝟏nk+𝜺k.subscript𝒚𝑘subscript𝐗𝑘subscript𝜷𝑘subscript𝜃𝑘subscript1subscript𝑛𝑘subscript𝜺𝑘\displaystyle\boldsymbol{y}_{k}=\mathbf{X}_{k}\boldsymbol{\beta}_{k}+{\theta}_{k}\mathbf{1}_{n_{k}}+\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{k}.

Then, for k=0,…,K𝑘0…𝐾k=0,\dots,K, we let θ^ksubscript^𝜃𝑘\hat{\theta}_{k} denote the least-squares estimator of θksubscript𝜃𝑘{\theta}_{k} in population Pksubscript𝑃𝑘P_{k} when regressed separately. Writing 𝐙k=(𝟏nk,𝐗k)∈ℝnk×(p+1)subscript𝐙𝑘subscript1subscript𝑛𝑘subscript𝐗𝑘superscriptℝsubscript𝑛𝑘𝑝1\mathbf{Z}_{k}=(\mathbf{1}_{n_{k}},\mathbf{X}_{k})\in\mathbb{R}^{n_{k}\times(p+1)} to denote the combined design matrix, we have that

[𝐈nk−𝐙k​(𝐙k⊤​𝐙k)−1​𝐙k⊤]​𝒚k∼𝒩​(0,σε2​[𝐈nk−𝐙k​(𝐙k⊤​𝐙k)−1​𝐙k⊤])similar-todelimited-[]subscript𝐈subscript𝑛𝑘subscript𝐙𝑘superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐙𝑘topsubscript𝐙𝑘1superscriptsubscript𝐙𝑘topsubscript𝒚𝑘𝒩0superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2delimited-[]subscript𝐈subscript𝑛𝑘subscript𝐙𝑘superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐙𝑘topsubscript𝐙𝑘1superscriptsubscript𝐙𝑘top\displaystyle[\mathbf{I}_{n_{k}}-\mathbf{Z}_{k}(\mathbf{Z}_{k}^{\top}\mathbf{Z}_{k})^{-1}\mathbf{Z}_{k}^{\top}]\boldsymbol{y}_{k}\sim\mathcal{N}\Big{(}0,\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}[\mathbf{I}_{n_{k}}-\mathbf{Z}_{k}(\mathbf{Z}_{k}^{\top}\mathbf{Z}_{k})^{-1}\mathbf{Z}_{k}^{\top}]\Big{)}

and

θ^k∼𝒩​(0,σπ2+σε2nk−𝟏nk​𝐗k​(𝐗k⊤​𝐗k)−1​𝐗k⊤​𝟏nk).similar-tosubscript^𝜃𝑘𝒩0superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2subscript𝑛𝑘subscript1subscript𝑛𝑘subscript𝐗𝑘superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐗𝑘topsubscript𝐗𝑘1superscriptsubscript𝐗𝑘topsubscript1subscript𝑛𝑘\displaystyle\hat{\theta}_{k}\sim\mathcal{N}\Big{(}0,\sigma_{\pi}^{2}+\frac{\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}}{n_{k}-\mathbf{1}_{n_{k}}\mathbf{X}_{k}(\mathbf{X}_{k}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{k})^{-1}\mathbf{X}_{k}^{\top}\mathbf{1}_{n_{k}}}\Big{)}.

Therefore, to estimate σπ2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2\sigma_{\pi}^{2} and σε2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}, we consider

σ^ε2≜∑k=1K‖[𝐈nk−𝐙k​(𝐙k⊤​𝐙k)−1​𝐙k⊤]​𝒚k‖22∑k=1Knk−K​p≜superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜀2superscriptsubscript𝑘1𝐾superscriptsubscriptnormdelimited-[]subscript𝐈subscript𝑛𝑘subscript𝐙𝑘superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐙𝑘topsubscript𝐙𝑘1superscriptsubscript𝐙𝑘topsubscript𝒚𝑘22superscriptsubscript𝑘1𝐾subscript𝑛𝑘𝐾𝑝\displaystyle\hat{\sigma}_{\varepsilon}^{2}\triangleq\frac{\sum_{k=1}^{K}\|[\mathbf{I}_{n_{k}}-\mathbf{Z}_{k}(\mathbf{Z}_{k}^{\top}\mathbf{Z}_{k})^{-1}\mathbf{Z}_{k}^{\top}]\boldsymbol{y}_{k}\|_{2}^{2}}{\sum_{k=1}^{K}n_{k}-Kp}

and

σ^π2≜1K​∑k=1K(θ^k2−σ^ε2nk−𝟏n0​𝐗k​(𝐗k⊤​𝐗k)−1​𝐗k⊤​𝟏n0).≜superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜋21𝐾superscriptsubscript𝑘1𝐾superscriptsubscript^𝜃𝑘2superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜀2subscript𝑛𝑘subscript1subscript𝑛0subscript𝐗𝑘superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐗𝑘topsubscript𝐗𝑘1superscriptsubscript𝐗𝑘topsubscript1subscript𝑛0\displaystyle\hat{\sigma}_{\pi}^{2}\triangleq\frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\Big{(}\hat{\theta}_{k}^{2}-\frac{\hat{\sigma}_{\varepsilon}^{2}}{n_{k}-\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}\mathbf{X}_{k}(\mathbf{X}_{k}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{k})^{-1}\mathbf{X}_{k}^{\top}\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}}\Big{)}.

Supposing σ^π2superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜋2\hat{\sigma}_{\pi}^{2} and σ^ε2superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜀2\hat{\sigma}_{\varepsilon}^{2} satisfy

  1. 1.

    σ^π2−σπ2=opr​(σπ2)superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜋2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2subscript𝑜prsuperscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2\hat{\sigma}_{\pi}^{2}-\sigma_{\pi}^{2}=o_{\operatorname{pr}}(\sigma_{\pi}^{2}); and

  2. 2.

    σ^ε2−σε2=opr​(σε2)superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜀2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2subscript𝑜prsuperscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2\hat{\sigma}_{\varepsilon}^{2}-\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}=o_{\operatorname{pr}}(\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2}),

the straightforward calculations imply that the empirical Bayes confidence interval is given by

ℐebsubscriptℐeb\displaystyle\mathcal{I}_{\text{eb}} =(σ^π2σ^π2+s2​θ^0±zα/2​σ^π2​s2σ^π2+s2),absentplus-or-minussuperscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜋2superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜋2superscript𝑠2subscript^𝜃0subscript𝑧𝛼2superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜋2superscript𝑠2superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜋2superscript𝑠2\displaystyle=\Big{(}\frac{\hat{\sigma}_{\pi}^{2}}{\hat{\sigma}_{\pi}^{2}+s^{2}}\hat{\theta}_{0}\pm z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{\hat{\sigma}_{\pi}^{2}s^{2}}{\hat{\sigma}_{\pi}^{2}+s^{2}}}\Big{)},

where

s2≜σ^ε2n0−𝟏n0​𝐗0​(∑k=0K𝐗k⊤​𝐗k)−1​𝐗0⊤​𝟏n0.≜superscript𝑠2superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜀2subscript𝑛0subscript1subscript𝑛0subscript𝐗0superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑘0𝐾superscriptsubscript𝐗𝑘topsubscript𝐗𝑘1superscriptsubscript𝐗0topsubscript1subscript𝑛0\displaystyle s^{2}\triangleq\frac{\hat{\sigma}_{\varepsilon}^{2}}{n_{0}-\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}\mathbf{X}_{0}(\sum_{k=0}^{K}\mathbf{X}_{k}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{k})^{-1}\mathbf{X}_{0}^{\top}\mathbf{1}_{n_{0}}}.

To show asymptotic validity, we verify Assumption A holds. Indeed, for an intermediate point ξ2superscript𝜉2\xi^{2} between σπ2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2\sigma_{\pi}^{2} and σ^π2superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜋2\hat{\sigma}_{\pi}^{2}, we have

‖π^​(⋅)−π​(⋅)‖1subscriptnorm^𝜋⋅𝜋⋅1\displaystyle\|\hat{\pi}(\cdot)-\pi(\cdot)\|_{1} =∫−∞∞|12​π​σ^π2​exp⁡(−θ022​σ^π2)−12​π​σπ2​exp⁡(−θ022​σπ2)|​λ​(d​θ)absentsuperscriptsubscript12𝜋superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜋2superscriptsubscript𝜃022superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜋212𝜋superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2superscriptsubscript𝜃022superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2𝜆𝑑𝜃\displaystyle=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\Big{|}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\hat{\sigma}_{\pi}^{2}}}\exp\Big{(}-\frac{\theta_{0}^{2}}{2\hat{\sigma}_{\pi}^{2}}\Big{)}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_{\pi}^{2}}}\exp\Big{(}-\frac{\theta_{0}^{2}}{2\sigma_{\pi}^{2}}\Big{)}\Big{|}\lambda(d\theta)
=∫−∞∞|θ022​ξ4−12​ξ2|​(σ^π2−σπ2)​12​π​σπ2​exp⁡(−θ022​σπ2)​λ​(d​θ)absentsuperscriptsubscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝜃022superscript𝜉412superscript𝜉2superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜋2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋212𝜋superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2superscriptsubscript𝜃022superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2𝜆𝑑𝜃\displaystyle=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\Big{|}\frac{\theta_{0}^{2}}{2\xi^{4}}-\frac{1}{2\xi^{2}}\Big{|}(\hat{\sigma}_{\pi}^{2}-\sigma_{\pi}^{2})\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_{\pi}^{2}}}\exp\Big{(}-\frac{\theta_{0}^{2}}{2\sigma_{\pi}^{2}}\Big{)}\lambda(d\theta)
≤|σπ2​(σ^π2−σπ2)2​ξ4|+|σ^π2−σπ22​ξ2|absentsuperscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜋2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋22superscript𝜉4superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜋2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋22superscript𝜉2\displaystyle\leq\Big{|}\frac{\sigma_{\pi}^{2}(\hat{\sigma}_{\pi}^{2}-\sigma_{\pi}^{2})}{2\xi^{4}}\Big{|}+\Big{|}\frac{\hat{\sigma}_{\pi}^{2}-\sigma_{\pi}^{2}}{2\xi^{2}}\Big{|}
=opr​(1).absentsubscript𝑜pr1\displaystyle=o_{\operatorname{pr}}(1).

By the exact same calculation, it follows that

supθ0∈ℝ∥f^n(⋅|θ0)−fn(⋅|θ0)∥1=opr(1).\displaystyle\sup_{\theta_{0}\in\mathbb{R}}\|\hat{f}_{n}(\cdot|\theta_{0})-f_{n}(\cdot|\theta_{0})\|_{1}=o_{\operatorname{pr}}(1).

Thus, we conclude that

lim infn→∞prθ^0,θ0⁡(ℐeb∋θ0)≥1−α.subscriptlimit-infimum→𝑛subscriptprsubscript^𝜃0subscript𝜃0subscript𝜃0subscriptℐeb1𝛼\displaystyle\liminf_{n\to\infty}\operatorname{pr}_{\hat{\theta}_{0},\theta_{0}}(\mathcal{I}_{\text{eb}}\ni\theta_{0})\geq 1-\alpha.

Note that the requirements on σ^π2superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜋2\hat{\sigma}_{\pi}^{2} and σ^ε2superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜀2\hat{\sigma}_{\varepsilon}^{2} are very mild. In the case where σπ2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2\sigma_{\pi}^{2} and σε2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2} are constant, we only require K→∞→𝐾K\to\infty. However, our calculations above also apply in the contiguous setting where n0​σπ2≍σε2asymptotically-equalssubscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜀2n_{0}\sigma_{\pi}^{2}\asymp\sigma_{\varepsilon}^{2} assuming that K→∞→𝐾K\to\infty sufficiently fast.

So far, we have only considered the asymptotic validity of our empirical Bayes confidence interval, though this raises the question of optimality; in particular, does the ratio of the Lebesgue measure between the empirical Bayes confidence region and the oracle Bayes confidence region converges to one? As the following example demonstrates, this is not true without further assumptions.

Example 2.

Consider the setting where n0=u0=0subscript𝑛0subscript𝑢00n_{0}=u_{0}=0 and πn​(⋅)subscript𝜋𝑛⋅\pi_{n}(\cdot) is the uniform density on (0,1)01(0,1). Suppose

π^n​(θ)={1+1n if ​θ∈(0,1−α);1+1n−1α​n if ​θ∈(1−α,1)subscript^𝜋𝑛𝜃cases11𝑛 if 𝜃01𝛼11𝑛1𝛼𝑛 if 𝜃1𝛼1\displaystyle\hat{\pi}_{n}(\theta)=\begin{cases}1+\frac{1}{n}&\text{ if }\theta\in(0,1-\alpha);\\ 1+\frac{1}{n}-\frac{1}{\alpha n}&\text{ if }\theta\in(1-\alpha,1)\end{cases}

for n≥1/α−1𝑛1𝛼1n\geq 1/\alpha-1. Then, it is clear that ‖π^n−πn‖1→0→subscriptnormsubscript^𝜋𝑛subscript𝜋𝑛10\|\hat{\pi}_{n}-\pi_{n}\|_{1}\to 0 since

∫01|π^n​(θ0)−πn​(θ0)|​λ​(d​θ0)=1−αn+1−αα2​n→0.superscriptsubscript01subscript^𝜋𝑛subscript𝜃0subscript𝜋𝑛subscript𝜃0𝜆𝑑subscript𝜃01𝛼𝑛1𝛼superscript𝛼2𝑛→0\displaystyle\int_{0}^{1}|\hat{\pi}_{n}(\theta_{0})-\pi_{n}(\theta_{0})|\lambda(d\theta_{0})=\frac{1-\alpha}{n}+\frac{1-\alpha}{\alpha^{2}n}\to 0.

However, we have ℐob=(0,1)subscriptℐob01\mathcal{I}_{\text{ob}}=(0,1) and ℐeb=(0,1−α)subscriptℐeb01𝛼\mathcal{I}_{\text{eb}}=(0,1-\alpha) for all n≥1/α−1𝑛1𝛼1n\geq 1/\alpha-1. Hence, it follows that

λ​(ℐob)λ​(ℐeb)=11−α.𝜆subscriptℐob𝜆subscriptℐeb11𝛼\displaystyle\frac{\lambda(\mathcal{I}_{\text{ob}})}{\lambda(\mathcal{I}_{\text{eb}})}=\frac{1}{1-\alpha}.

5 Asymptotically Linear Estimators

In this section, we consider the setting where the statistic 𝐔0subscript𝐔0\mathbf{U}_{0} is an asymptotically linear estimator θ^0subscript^𝜃0\hat{\theta}_{0} of θ0subscript𝜃0\theta_{0}. We note that this is a very general setting as most classical confidence intervals are constructed through an asymptotic Gaussian pivot, which usually are asymptotically linear. Following the notation of ? (?), for k=0,1,…,K𝑘01…𝐾k=0,1,\dots,K, we assume that

θ~k=θk+1nk​∑i=1nkψ​(Xi,k;θk,ηk)+δk.subscript~𝜃𝑘subscript𝜃𝑘1subscript𝑛𝑘superscriptsubscript𝑖1subscript𝑛𝑘𝜓subscript𝑋𝑖𝑘subscript𝜃𝑘subscript𝜂𝑘subscript𝛿𝑘\displaystyle\tilde{\theta}_{k}={\theta}_{k}+\frac{1}{n_{k}}\sum_{i=1}^{n_{k}}\psi(X_{i,k};{\theta}_{k},{\eta}_{k})+{\delta}_{k}.

Here, ψ​(⋅;θk,ηk)𝜓⋅subscript𝜃𝑘subscript𝜂𝑘\psi(\cdot;{\theta}_{k},{\eta}_{k}) is the influence function, ηk∈H⊆ℝsubscript𝜂𝑘𝐻ℝ{\eta}_{k}\in H\subseteq\mathbb{R} is a nuisance parameter, and δk=opr​(nk−1/2)subscript𝛿𝑘subscript𝑜prsuperscriptsubscript𝑛𝑘12{\delta}_{k}=o_{\operatorname{pr}}(n_{k}^{-1/2}) is a small remainder term. As we formalize in Assumption C below, we assume that ψ​(⋅;θk,ηk)𝜓⋅subscript𝜃𝑘subscript𝜂𝑘\psi(\cdot;{\theta}_{k},{\eta}_{k}) is asymptotically independent for θksubscript𝜃𝑘{\theta}_{k}; in particular, the asymptotic variance does not depend on θksubscript𝜃𝑘{\theta}_{k}.

For ease of presentation, we start by deriving results for a generic asymptotically linear estimator of θ(n)∼πnsimilar-tosuperscript𝜃𝑛subscript𝜋𝑛\theta^{(n)}\sim\pi_{n} based on n𝑛n independent and identically distributed observations Xi∼Pθ(n)similar-tosubscript𝑋𝑖subscript𝑃superscript𝜃𝑛X_{i}\sim P_{\theta^{(n)}} given by

θ~(n)=θ(n)+1n​∑i=1nψ​(Xi;θ(n),η)+δn.superscript~𝜃𝑛superscript𝜃𝑛1𝑛superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑛𝜓subscript𝑋𝑖superscript𝜃𝑛𝜂subscript𝛿𝑛\displaystyle\tilde{\theta}^{(n)}=\theta^{(n)}+\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\psi(X_{i};\theta^{(n)},\eta)+\delta_{n}.

In general, the distribution of θ~(n)|θ(n)conditionalsuperscript~𝜃𝑛superscript𝜃𝑛\tilde{\theta}^{(n)}|\theta^{(n)} is intractable or difficult to compute. However, due to the asymptotic linearity, assuming that ψ​(⋅;θ(n),η)𝜓⋅superscript𝜃𝑛𝜂\psi(\cdot;\theta^{(n)},\eta) has a second moment, we have from the central limit theorem that there exists a variance σ2superscript𝜎2\sigma^{2} such that

n​(θ~(n)−θ(n))|θ(n)​→𝑑​𝒩​(0,σ2).conditional𝑛superscript~𝜃𝑛superscript𝜃𝑛superscript𝜃𝑛𝑑→𝒩0superscript𝜎2\displaystyle\sqrt{n}(\tilde{\theta}^{(n)}-\theta^{(n)})|\theta^{(n)}\overset{d}{\to}\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^{2}).

Then, rather than directly using the distribution of θ~(n)superscript~𝜃𝑛\tilde{\theta}^{(n)}, we may wish to approximate it with its asymptotic distribution. We emphasize that naively substituting the Gaussian distribution in place of the true distribution does not satisfy Assumption A.

To see this, we recall a result of ? (?) (see also ? (?)), which asserts that the central limit theorem holds in total variation distance if and only if the distribution of the partial sum of the first n𝑛n terms is non-singular for some n𝑛n. If the distribution of ∑i=1nψ​(Xi;θ(n),η)superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑛𝜓subscript𝑋𝑖superscript𝜃𝑛𝜂\sum_{i=1}^{n}\psi(X_{i};\theta^{(n)},\eta) is always singular and δn=0subscript𝛿𝑛0\delta_{n}=0 almost surely — for example, the sample mean of a discrete distribution — then the total variation distance between θ~(n)|θ(n)conditionalsuperscript~𝜃𝑛superscript𝜃𝑛\tilde{\theta}^{(n)}|\theta^{(n)} and the Gaussian distribution is always one. In this case, the asymptotic coverage arising from these posterior level sets does not attain the nominal 1−α1𝛼1-\alpha level.

Thus, for technical reasons, we have to slightly modify our estimator to be

θ^(n)≜θ~(n)+1n​ξn,≜superscript^𝜃𝑛superscript~𝜃𝑛1𝑛subscript𝜉𝑛\displaystyle\hat{\theta}^{(n)}\triangleq\tilde{\theta}^{(n)}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\xi_{n},

where ξn∼𝒩​(0,ςn2)similar-tosubscript𝜉𝑛𝒩0subscriptsuperscript𝜍2𝑛\xi_{n}\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\varsigma^{2}_{n}) for some sequence of positive constants {ςn2}n=1∞superscriptsubscriptsubscriptsuperscript𝜍2𝑛𝑛1\{\varsigma^{2}_{n}\}_{n=1}^{\infty} decreasing to zero slowly (see Proposition 3 below). Even if the distribution of ψ​(Xi;θ(n),η)𝜓subscript𝑋𝑖superscript𝜃𝑛𝜂\psi(X_{i};\theta^{(n)},\eta) is singular, by adding independent Gaussian noise, the infinite divisibility property of the Gaussian distribution implies that the above display is again a partial sum of independent and identically distributed random variables, but now with a non-singular component. Therefore, the addition of ξn/nsubscript𝜉𝑛𝑛\xi_{n}/\sqrt{n} produces a smoothing effect on the resultant estimator to allow for convergence in total variation. However, the sequence {ςn2}n=1∞superscriptsubscriptsubscriptsuperscript𝜍2𝑛𝑛1\{\varsigma^{2}_{n}\}_{n=1}^{\infty} cannot tend to zero too quickly to ensure a sufficient amount of smoothing.

We start with a simple sufficient condition to approximate the density of n​θ^(n)|θ(n)conditional𝑛superscript^𝜃𝑛superscript𝜃𝑛\sqrt{n}\hat{\theta}^{(n)}|\theta^{(n)}.

Assumption B.

The influence function satisfies

supθ∈Υ𝔼​[ψ3​(Xi;θ,η)]<∞subscriptsupremum𝜃Υ𝔼delimited-[]superscript𝜓3subscript𝑋𝑖𝜃𝜂\displaystyle\sup_{\theta\in\Upsilon}\mathbb{E}[\psi^{3}(X_{i};\theta,\eta)]<\infty

for all compacts Υ⊆ΘΥΘ\Upsilon\subseteq\Theta. Moreover, the densities {fn(⋅|θ)}n=1∞\{f_{n}(\cdot|\theta)\}_{n=1}^{\infty} are bounded and continuous functions of both arguments.

Assumption B is a mild assumption to ensure uniform convergence of the central limit theorem over compact subsets Υ⊆ΘΥΘ\Upsilon\subseteq\Theta. If the third moment is finite and continuous as a function of θ𝜃\theta, then the above condition holds.

Example 3 (Ordinary least-sqares and debiased lasso).

As an example of an estimator satisfying Assumption B, we consider the linear model

𝒚k=𝐗k​𝜷k+𝜺k.subscript𝒚𝑘subscript𝐗𝑘subscript𝜷𝑘subscript𝜺𝑘\displaystyle\boldsymbol{y}_{k}=\mathbf{X}_{k}\boldsymbol{\beta}_{k}+\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{k}.

where 𝒚k,𝜺k∈ℝnksubscript𝒚𝑘subscript𝜺𝑘superscriptℝsubscript𝑛𝑘\boldsymbol{y}_{k},\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{k}\in\mathbb{R}^{n_{k}}, 𝐗∈ℝnk×p𝐗superscriptℝsubscript𝑛𝑘𝑝\mathbf{X}\in\mathbb{R}^{n_{k}\times p}, and 𝜷k∈ℝpsubscript𝜷𝑘superscriptℝ𝑝\boldsymbol{\beta}_{k}\in\mathbb{R}^{p}. Moreover, our parameter of interest is θk=𝜷k,1subscript𝜃𝑘subscript𝜷𝑘1{\theta}_{k}=\boldsymbol{\beta}_{k,1}, the first entry of 𝜷ksubscript𝜷𝑘\boldsymbol{\beta}_{k}. Then, in the low-dimensional setting, the least-squares estimator is both asymptotically linear with

𝜷^k=𝜷k+1nk​∑i=1nk(𝔼​𝐗k⊤​𝐗k)−1​𝒙k,i​εk,i+opr​(nk−1);subscript^𝜷𝑘subscript𝜷𝑘1subscript𝑛𝑘superscriptsubscript𝑖1subscript𝑛𝑘superscript𝔼superscriptsubscript𝐗𝑘topsubscript𝐗𝑘1subscript𝒙𝑘𝑖subscript𝜀𝑘𝑖subscript𝑜prsuperscriptsubscript𝑛𝑘1\displaystyle\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{k}=\boldsymbol{\beta}_{k}+\frac{1}{n_{k}}\sum_{i=1}^{n_{k}}(\mathbb{E}\mathbf{X}_{k}^{\top}\mathbf{X}_{k})^{-1}\boldsymbol{x}_{k,i}\varepsilon_{k,i}+o_{\operatorname{pr}}(n_{k}^{-1});

for example, see page 35 of ? (?). Similarly, ? (?) show that the debiased lasso has the same influence function as the least-squares estimator restricted to the sub-model spanned by the active covariates. In both cases, as long as the distribution of ε𝜀\varepsilon admits a finite third moment, Assumption B is satisfied. Moreover, if 𝜺ksubscript𝜺𝑘\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{k} has a distribution that is absolutely continuous with respect to Lebesgue measure, such as the Gaussian distribution, then no smoothing is required for the least-squares estimator since 𝜷^ksubscript^𝜷𝑘\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{k} is non-singular.

Letting fn(⋅|θ(n))f_{n}(\cdot|\theta^{(n)}) denote the density of n​θ^(n)|θ(n)conditional𝑛superscript^𝜃𝑛superscript𝜃𝑛\sqrt{n}\hat{\theta}^{(n)}|\theta^{(n)}, the following proposition shows that fn(⋅|θ(n))f_{n}(\cdot|\theta^{(n)}) converges in total variation distance to φ(⋅|nθ(n),σ2)\varphi(\cdot|\sqrt{n}\theta^{(n)},\sigma^{2}).

Proposition 3.

If Assumption B holds, then there exists a smoothing sequence {ςn2}n=1∞superscriptsubscriptsubscriptsuperscript𝜍2𝑛𝑛1\{\varsigma^{2}_{n}\}_{n=1}^{\infty} such that

supθ∈Υ∥fn(⋅|θ(n))−φ(⋅|nθ(n),σ2)∥1=o(1)\displaystyle\sup_{\theta\in\Upsilon}\|f_{n}(\cdot|\theta^{(n)})-\varphi(\cdot|\sqrt{n}\theta^{(n)},\sigma^{2})\|_{1}=o(1)

for all compacts Υ⊆ΘΥΘ\Upsilon\subseteq\Theta.

Proposition 3 shows that, for a suitable regularizing sequence {ςn2}n=1∞superscriptsubscriptsubscriptsuperscript𝜍2𝑛𝑛1\{\varsigma^{2}_{n}\}_{n=1}^{\infty}, the distribution of θ^(n)|θ(n)conditionalsuperscript^𝜃𝑛superscript𝜃𝑛\hat{\theta}^{(n)}|\theta^{(n)} can be well approximated by a Gaussian distribution in the sense of Assumption A. Since σ2superscript𝜎2\sigma^{2} is unknown, it must be estimated from the data; however, in general, a consistent estimator of σ2superscript𝜎2\sigma^{2} is used to construct classical confidence intervals based on the asymptotic distribution of θ~(n)superscript~𝜃𝑛\tilde{\theta}^{(n)}. In this case, we have the following corollary.

Corollary 3.1.

Under the setting of Proposition 3, if σ^2/σ2​→𝑃​1superscript^𝜎2superscript𝜎2𝑃→1\hat{\sigma}^{2}/\sigma^{2}\overset{P}{\to}1, then

supθ∈Υ∥fn(⋅|θ(n))−φ(⋅|nθ(n),σ^2)∥1=opr(1).\displaystyle\sup_{\theta\in\Upsilon}\|f_{n}(\cdot|\theta^{(n)})-\varphi(\cdot|\sqrt{n}\theta^{(n)},\hat{\sigma}^{2})\|_{1}=o_{\operatorname{pr}}(1).

Now that we have found a suitable approximation of fn(⋅|θ(n))f_{n}(\cdot|\theta^{(n)}), it is left to approximate the distribution πn​(⋅)subscript𝜋𝑛⋅\pi_{n}(\cdot). We may again leverage the asymptotic linearity structure of θ^(n)superscript^𝜃𝑛\hat{\theta}^{(n)}. If ψ​(⋅;θ(n),η)𝜓⋅superscript𝜃𝑛𝜂\psi(\cdot;\theta^{(n)},\eta) is asymptotically independent of θ(n)superscript𝜃𝑛\theta^{(n)}, then θ^(n)superscript^𝜃𝑛\hat{\theta}^{(n)} is approximately θ(n)superscript𝜃𝑛\theta^{(n)} plus an independent Gaussian term, implying that the marginal distribution of θ^(n)superscript^𝜃𝑛\hat{\theta}^{(n)} is close to πnsubscript𝜋𝑛\pi_{n} convolved with the Gaussian distribution. Before formalizing this intuition, for a scaling sequence ansubscript𝑎𝑛a_{n}, we let νnsubscript𝜈𝑛\nu_{n} denote the distribution of an​(θ(n)−𝔼​θ(n))subscript𝑎𝑛superscript𝜃𝑛𝔼superscript𝜃𝑛a_{n}(\theta^{(n)}-\mathbb{E}\theta^{(n)}) and make the following assumption.

Assumption C.

The density νn​(⋅)subscript𝜈𝑛⋅\nu_{n}(\cdot) converges in L2superscript𝐿2L^{2} to a continuous density ν∞​(⋅)subscript𝜈⋅\nu_{\infty}(\cdot). Moreover, ψ​(⋅;θ(n),η)𝜓⋅superscript𝜃𝑛𝜂\psi(\cdot;\theta^{(n)},\eta) is asymptotically independent of θ(n)superscript𝜃𝑛\theta^{(n)}.

Letting qn​(⋅)subscript𝑞𝑛⋅q_{n}(\cdot) be the marginal density of an​(θ^(n)−𝔼​θ(n))subscript𝑎𝑛superscript^𝜃𝑛𝔼superscript𝜃𝑛a_{n}(\hat{\theta}^{(n)}-\mathbb{E}\theta^{(n)}), we have the following proposition.

Proposition 4.

Suppose Assumption C holds.

  1. 1.

    If an=o​(n)subscript𝑎𝑛𝑜𝑛a_{n}=o(\sqrt{n}), then there exists a smoothing sequence {ςn2}n=1∞superscriptsubscriptsubscriptsuperscript𝜍2𝑛𝑛1\{\varsigma^{2}_{n}\}_{n=1}^{\infty} such that

    ‖qn−ν∞‖2→0.→subscriptnormsubscript𝑞𝑛subscript𝜈20\displaystyle\|q_{n}-\nu_{\infty}\|_{2}\to 0.
  2. 2.

    If an=nsubscript𝑎𝑛𝑛a_{n}=\sqrt{n}, then there exists a smoothing sequence {ςn2}n=1∞superscriptsubscriptsubscriptsuperscript𝜍2𝑛𝑛1\{\varsigma^{2}_{n}\}_{n=1}^{\infty} such that

    ‖qn−ν∞∗φ0,σ2‖2→0.→subscriptnormsubscript𝑞𝑛∗subscript𝜈subscript𝜑0superscript𝜎220\displaystyle\|q_{n}-\nu_{\infty}\ast\varphi_{0,\sigma^{2}}\|_{2}\to 0.

In both cases, the convergence also holds in L1superscript𝐿1L^{1}.

Proposition 4 asserts that the marginal density of θ^(n)superscript^𝜃𝑛\hat{\theta}^{(n)} is close to the prior density πnsubscript𝜋𝑛\pi_{n} or the convolution of πnsubscript𝜋𝑛\pi_{n} with a Gaussian density, depending on the signal-to-noise ratio regime. In the subsequent two subsections, we leverage this result to construct estimators of πn​(⋅)subscript𝜋𝑛⋅\pi_{n}(\cdot) depending on whether πn​(⋅)subscript𝜋𝑛⋅\pi_{n}(\cdot) is assumed to be Gaussian or not.

5.1 Gaussian Empirical Bayes

In this subsection, we generalize the Gaussian one-way random effects model from Section 2 and assume that πn(⋅)=πn(⋅|μπ,σπ2)\pi_{n}(\cdot)=\pi_{n}(\cdot|\mu_{\pi},\sigma_{\pi}^{2}) is a Gaussian distribution with mean μπsubscript𝜇𝜋\mu_{\pi} and variance σπ2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2\sigma_{\pi}^{2}. Here, σπ2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2\sigma_{\pi}^{2} may change with n𝑛n, enabling a contiguous prior, but we omit this dependence for simplicity. From Proposition 4, we have that θ^(n)superscript^𝜃𝑛\hat{\theta}^{(n)} has an approximate marginal distribution of 𝒩​(μπ,σπ2+σ2/n)𝒩subscript𝜇𝜋superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2superscript𝜎2𝑛\mathcal{N}(\mu_{\pi},\sigma_{\pi}^{2}+\sigma^{2}/n). Since the K𝐾K populations are independent, we may consider the maximum likelihood estimators given by

(μ~π,σ~π2)≜arg​min(μπ,σπ2)∈ℝ×ℝ+⁡{∏k=1Kφ​(θ^k|μπ,σπ2+σ^2/nk)}.≜subscript~𝜇𝜋subscriptsuperscript~𝜎2𝜋subscriptargminsubscript𝜇𝜋superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2ℝsubscriptℝsuperscriptsubscriptproduct𝑘1𝐾𝜑conditionalsubscript^𝜃𝑘subscript𝜇𝜋superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2superscript^𝜎2subscript𝑛𝑘\displaystyle(\tilde{\mu}_{\pi},\tilde{\sigma}^{2}_{\pi})\triangleq\operatorname*{arg\,min}_{(\mu_{\pi},\sigma_{\pi}^{2})\in\mathbb{R}\times\mathbb{R}_{+}}\Big{\{}\prod_{k=1}^{K}\varphi(\hat{\theta}_{k}|\mu_{\pi},\sigma_{\pi}^{2}+\hat{\sigma}^{2}/n_{k})\Big{\}}.

which reduces to

μ~π≜1K​∑k=1Kθ^kandσ~π2≜max⁡{1K​∑k=1K(θ^k−μ^π)2−σ^2m,0}formulae-sequence≜subscript~𝜇𝜋1𝐾superscriptsubscript𝑘1𝐾subscript^𝜃𝑘and≜subscriptsuperscript~𝜎2𝜋1𝐾superscriptsubscript𝑘1𝐾superscriptsubscript^𝜃𝑘subscript^𝜇𝜋2superscript^𝜎2𝑚0\displaystyle\tilde{\mu}_{\pi}\triangleq\frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\hat{\theta}_{k}\hskip 20.00003pt\text{and}\hskip 20.00003pt\tilde{\sigma}^{2}_{\pi}\triangleq\max\Big{\{}\frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K}(\hat{\theta}_{k}-\hat{\mu}_{\pi})^{2}-\frac{\hat{\sigma}^{2}}{m},0\Big{\}}

when m=n1=⋯=nK𝑚subscript𝑛1⋯subscript𝑛𝐾m=n_{1}=\dots=n_{K}. However, if σ~π2=0subscriptsuperscript~𝜎2𝜋0\tilde{\sigma}^{2}_{\pi}=0, then the resultant posterior is a point mass and the empirical Bayes confidence region is the singleton μ~πsubscript~𝜇𝜋\tilde{\mu}_{\pi} regardless of θ^0subscript^𝜃0\hat{\theta}_{0}. To avoid this problem, we take a regularizing sequence {ζn2}n=1∞superscriptsubscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝜁𝑛2𝑛1\{\zeta_{n}^{2}\}_{n=1}^{\infty} satisfying ζn2=o​(σπ2)superscriptsubscript𝜁𝑛2𝑜superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2\zeta_{n}^{2}=o(\sigma_{\pi}^{2}) and consider

μ^π≜μ~πandσ^π2≜max⁡{σ~π2,ζn2}.formulae-sequence≜subscript^𝜇𝜋subscript~𝜇𝜋and≜superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜋2subscriptsuperscript~𝜎2𝜋superscriptsubscript𝜁𝑛2\displaystyle\hat{\mu}_{\pi}\triangleq\tilde{\mu}_{\pi}\hskip 20.00003pt\text{and}\hskip 20.00003pt\hat{\sigma}_{\pi}^{2}\triangleq\max\{\tilde{\sigma}^{2}_{\pi},\zeta_{n}^{2}\}.

In both cases, we estimate πn​(⋅)subscript𝜋𝑛⋅\pi_{n}(\cdot) by π^n(⋅)=φ(⋅|μ^π,σ^π2)\hat{\pi}_{n}(\cdot)=\varphi(\cdot|\hat{\mu}_{\pi},\hat{\sigma}_{\pi}^{2}), leading to the estimated empirical Bayes confidence interval as

ℐeb≜(n0​σ^π2σ^2+n0​σ^π2​θ^0+σ^2σ^2+n0​σ^π2​μ^π±zα/2​σ^π2​σ^2σ^2+n0​σ^π2).≜subscriptℐebplus-or-minussubscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜋2superscript^𝜎2subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜋2subscript^𝜃0superscript^𝜎2superscript^𝜎2subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜋2subscript^𝜇𝜋subscript𝑧𝛼2superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜋2superscript^𝜎2superscript^𝜎2subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜋2\displaystyle\begin{aligned} \mathcal{I}_{\text{eb}}\triangleq\Big{(}\frac{n_{0}\hat{\sigma}_{\pi}^{2}}{\hat{\sigma}^{2}+n_{0}\hat{\sigma}_{\pi}^{2}}\hat{\theta}_{0}+\frac{\hat{\sigma}^{2}}{\hat{\sigma}^{2}+n_{0}\hat{\sigma}_{\pi}^{2}}\hat{\mu}_{\pi}\pm z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{\hat{\sigma}_{\pi}^{2}\hat{\sigma}^{2}}{\hat{\sigma}^{2}+n_{0}\hat{\sigma}_{\pi}^{2}}}\Big{)}.\end{aligned} (4)

This interval should be compared with the classical large sample confidence interval arising from using θ~0subscript~𝜃0\tilde{\theta}_{0} as an asymptotic pivot,

ℐcl≜(θ~0±zα/2​σ^2n0).≜subscriptℐclplus-or-minussubscript~𝜃0subscript𝑧𝛼2superscript^𝜎2subscript𝑛0\displaystyle\mathcal{I}_{\text{cl}}\triangleq\Big{(}\tilde{\theta}_{0}\pm z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{\hat{\sigma}^{2}}{n_{0}}}\Big{)}.

Under the following mild assumption, the above interval in equation (4) attains asymptotic coverage of the parameter θ0subscript𝜃0\theta_{0} and has strictly smaller Lebesgue measure than the classical large sample interval.

Assumption D.

The following three conditions hold:

  1. 1.

    m,K→∞→𝑚𝐾m,K\to\infty;

  2. 2.

    maxk=1,…,K⁡|δk|=opr​(σπ)subscript𝑘1…𝐾subscript𝛿𝑘subscript𝑜prsubscript𝜎𝜋\max_{k=1,\dots,K}|{\delta}_{k}|=o_{\operatorname{pr}}(\sigma_{\pi}); and

  3. 3.

    max⁡{σ^2−σ2,σ2}=opr​(m​σπ2)superscript^𝜎2superscript𝜎2superscript𝜎2subscript𝑜pr𝑚superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2\max\{\hat{\sigma}^{2}-\sigma^{2},\sigma^{2}\}=o_{\operatorname{pr}}(m\sigma_{\pi}^{2}).

Theorem 1.

Suppose Assumptions B, C, and D hold. If πn(⋅)=φ(⋅|μπ,σπ2)\pi_{n}(\cdot)=\varphi(\cdot|\mu_{\pi},\sigma_{\pi}^{2}), then

‖φμπ,σπ2−φμ^π,σ^π2‖1=opr​(1).subscriptnormsubscript𝜑subscript𝜇𝜋superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋2subscript𝜑subscript^𝜇𝜋superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜋21subscript𝑜pr1\displaystyle\|\varphi_{\mu_{\pi},\sigma_{\pi}^{2}}-\varphi_{\hat{\mu}_{\pi},\hat{\sigma}_{\pi}^{2}}\|_{1}=o_{\operatorname{pr}}(1).

Moreover, the interval defined in equation (4) satisfies

lim infn→∞prθ^0,θ0⁡(ℐeb∋θ0)≥1−αsubscriptlimit-infimum→𝑛subscriptprsubscript^𝜃0subscript𝜃0subscript𝜃0subscriptℐeb1𝛼\displaystyle\liminf_{n\to\infty}\operatorname{pr}_{\hat{\theta}_{0},\theta_{0}}(\mathcal{I}_{\text{eb}}\ni\theta_{0})\geq 1-\alpha

with

λ​(ℐeb)λ​(ℐcl)=1−n0​σ^π2σ^2+n0​σ^π2.𝜆subscriptℐeb𝜆subscriptℐcl1subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜋2superscript^𝜎2subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜋2\displaystyle\frac{\lambda(\mathcal{I}_{\text{eb}})}{\lambda(\mathcal{I}_{\text{cl}})}=1-\sqrt{\frac{n_{0}\hat{\sigma}_{\pi}^{2}}{\hat{\sigma}^{2}+n_{0}\hat{\sigma}_{\pi}^{2}}}.

5.2 Nonparametric Empirical Bayes

In this subsection, we consider the more general problem when πn​(⋅)subscript𝜋𝑛⋅\pi_{n}(\cdot) is a general nonparametric prior (cf. ? (?)). From Proposition 4, there are two distinct settings depending on the scaling rate ansubscript𝑎𝑛a_{n}. When an=o​(n)subscript𝑎𝑛𝑜𝑛a_{n}=o(\sqrt{n}), the noise of θ^(n)superscript^𝜃𝑛\hat{\theta}^{(n)} to θ(n)superscript𝜃𝑛\theta^{(n)} is asymptotically negligible relative to the strength of the signal. Thus, in this case, for a bandwidth parameter {bn}n=1∞superscriptsubscriptsubscript𝑏𝑛𝑛1\{b_{n}\}_{n=1}^{\infty} decreasing to zero, let

π^n​(t)=1K​∑k=1Kφ​(t|θ^k,bn2).subscript^𝜋𝑛𝑡1𝐾superscriptsubscript𝑘1𝐾𝜑conditional𝑡subscript^𝜃𝑘superscriptsubscript𝑏𝑛2\displaystyle\hat{\pi}_{n}(t)=\frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\varphi(t|\hat{\theta}_{k},b_{n}^{2}).

In this case, the posterior distribution of θ0|θ^0conditionalsubscript𝜃0subscript^𝜃0\theta_{0}|\hat{\theta}_{0} can be computed explicitly as the estimated prior is a Gaussian mixture. For k=1,…,K𝑘1…𝐾k=1,\dots,K, let

γk​(θ^0)≜φ​(θ^0|θ^k,bn2)∑j=1Kφ​(θ^0|θ^j,bn2).≜subscript𝛾𝑘subscript^𝜃0𝜑conditionalsubscript^𝜃0subscript^𝜃𝑘superscriptsubscript𝑏𝑛2superscriptsubscript𝑗1𝐾𝜑conditionalsubscript^𝜃0subscript^𝜃𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑏𝑛2\displaystyle{\gamma}_{k}(\hat{\theta}_{0})\triangleq\frac{\varphi(\hat{\theta}_{0}|\hat{\theta}_{k},b_{n}^{2})}{\sum_{j=1}^{K}\varphi(\hat{\theta}_{0}|\hat{\theta}_{j},b_{n}^{2})}.

Then, the posterior density of θ0|θ^0conditionalsubscript𝜃0subscript^𝜃0\theta_{0}|\hat{\theta}_{0} is

π^n​(x|θ^0)=∑k=1Kγk​(θ^0)​φ​(x|σ^2n0​bn2+σ^2​θ^k+n0​bn2n0​bn2+σ^2​θ^0,bn2​σ^2n0​bn2+σ^2).subscript^𝜋𝑛conditional𝑥subscript^𝜃0superscriptsubscript𝑘1𝐾subscript𝛾𝑘subscript^𝜃0𝜑conditional𝑥superscript^𝜎2subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript𝑏𝑛2superscript^𝜎2subscript^𝜃𝑘subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript𝑏𝑛2subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript𝑏𝑛2superscript^𝜎2subscript^𝜃0superscriptsubscript𝑏𝑛2superscript^𝜎2subscript𝑛0superscriptsubscript𝑏𝑛2superscript^𝜎2\displaystyle\hat{\pi}_{n}(x|\hat{\theta}_{0})=\sum_{k=1}^{K}{\gamma}_{k}(\hat{\theta}_{0})\varphi\Big{(}x\Big{|}\frac{\hat{\sigma}^{2}}{n_{0}b_{n}^{2}+\hat{\sigma}^{2}}\hat{\theta}_{k}+\frac{n_{0}b_{n}^{2}}{n_{0}b_{n}^{2}+\hat{\sigma}^{2}}\hat{\theta}_{0},\frac{b_{n}^{2}\hat{\sigma}^{2}}{n_{0}b_{n}^{2}+\hat{\sigma}^{2}}\Big{)}.

On the other hand, when an=nsubscript𝑎𝑛𝑛a_{n}=\sqrt{n}, the signal and the noise are of comparable magnitude and, hence, the contribution due to the noise needs to be removed. Therefore, following ? (?), we consider a deconvolution estimator

π~n​(x)=12​π​K​∑k=1K∫−1/bn1/bnexp⁡(−𝔧​x​z)​exp⁡(𝔧​z​θ^k)​exp⁡(σ^2​z22​m)​λ​(d​z)subscript~𝜋𝑛𝑥12𝜋𝐾superscriptsubscript𝑘1𝐾superscriptsubscript1subscript𝑏𝑛1subscript𝑏𝑛𝔧𝑥𝑧𝔧𝑧subscript^𝜃𝑘superscript^𝜎2superscript𝑧22𝑚𝜆𝑑𝑧\displaystyle\tilde{\pi}_{n}(x)=\frac{1}{2\pi K}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\int_{-1/b_{n}}^{1/b_{n}}\exp(-\mathfrak{j}xz)\exp(\mathfrak{j}z\hat{\theta}_{k})\exp\Big{(}\frac{\hat{\sigma}^{2}z^{2}}{2m}\Big{)}\lambda(dz)

where 𝔧𝔧\mathfrak{j} is the imaginary unit. To ensure convergence of the deconvolution estimator, we impose the following standard condition.

Assumption E.

The Fourier transforms {νnft}n=1∞superscriptsubscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝜈𝑛ft𝑛1\{\nu_{n}^{\text{ft}}\}_{n=1}^{\infty} and ν∞ftsuperscriptsubscript𝜈ft\nu_{\infty}^{\text{ft}} are integrable.

However, since the above estimator uses the Fourier transform of the sinc kernel, it may be negative on a set of positive measure. Therefore, the usual approach is to take the maximum with zero and renormalize the resultant density. Though it is consistent asymptotically, in finite samples, this leads to the undesirable property that, even if θ^0subscript^𝜃0\hat{\theta}_{0} is very close to θ0subscript𝜃0\theta_{0}. In particular, if the estimated prior is zero in a neighborhood of θ0subscript𝜃0\theta_{0}, the posterior is also zero in a neighborhood of θ0subscript𝜃0\theta_{0} and, hence, the empirical Bayes confidence region does not cover the parameter. Thus, for a regularizing sequence of convex weights {κn}n=1∞superscriptsubscriptsubscript𝜅𝑛𝑛1\{\kappa_{n}\}_{n=1}^{\infty} with κn→1→subscript𝜅𝑛1\kappa_{n}\to 1, we consider

π^n​(x)=κn​max⁡{π~n​(x),0}+(1−κn)​φ​(x|μ^π,σ^π2).subscript^𝜋𝑛𝑥subscript𝜅𝑛subscript~𝜋𝑛𝑥01subscript𝜅𝑛𝜑conditional𝑥subscript^𝜇𝜋superscriptsubscript^𝜎𝜋2\displaystyle\hat{\pi}_{n}(x)=\kappa_{n}\max\{\tilde{\pi}_{n}(x),0\}+(1-\kappa_{n})\varphi(x|\hat{\mu}_{\pi},\hat{\sigma}_{\pi}^{2}).

Then, the posterior density is solved by numerical integration.

In both cases, we construct the empirical Bayes confidence region as in equation (2), yielding

ℐeb≜{θ∈Θ:π^​(θ|θ^0)>τ^}.≜subscriptℐebconditional-set𝜃Θ^𝜋conditional𝜃subscript^𝜃0^𝜏\displaystyle\mathcal{I}_{\text{eb}}\triangleq\Big{\{}\theta\in\Theta:\hat{\pi}(\theta|\hat{\theta}_{0})>\hat{\tau}\Big{\}}. (5)

For this region, we have the following theorem.

Theorem 2.

Suppose Assumptions B, C, and D(1) hold.

  1. 1.

    If, in addition, an=o​(n)subscript𝑎𝑛𝑜𝑛a_{n}=o(\sqrt{n}), an2​bn2→0→superscriptsubscript𝑎𝑛2superscriptsubscript𝑏𝑛20a_{n}^{2}b_{n}^{2}\to 0, bn2→0→superscriptsubscript𝑏𝑛20b_{n}^{2}\to 0, and K​bn→∞→𝐾subscript𝑏𝑛Kb_{n}\to\infty, then

    ‖π^n−πn‖1=opr​(1).subscriptnormsubscript^𝜋𝑛subscript𝜋𝑛1subscript𝑜pr1\displaystyle\|\hat{\pi}_{n}-\pi_{n}\|_{1}=o_{\operatorname{pr}}(1).
  2. 2.

    If, in addition, Assumption E holds, then there exists a sequence {bn}n=1∞superscriptsubscriptsubscript𝑏𝑛𝑛1\{b_{n}\}_{n=1}^{\infty} with bn​n→0→subscript𝑏𝑛𝑛0b_{n}\sqrt{n}\to 0 such that

    ‖π^n−πn‖1=opr​(1).subscriptnormsubscript^𝜋𝑛subscript𝜋𝑛1subscript𝑜pr1\displaystyle\|\hat{\pi}_{n}-\pi_{n}\|_{1}=o_{\operatorname{pr}}(1).

In both settings, the region defined in equation (5) satisfies

lim infn→∞prθ^0,θ0⁡(ℐeb∋θ0)≥1−α.subscriptlimit-infimum→𝑛subscriptprsubscript^𝜃0subscript𝜃0subscript𝜃0subscriptℐeb1𝛼\displaystyle\liminf_{n\to\infty}\operatorname{pr}_{\hat{\theta}_{0},\theta_{0}}(\mathcal{I}_{\text{eb}}\ni\theta_{0})\geq 1-\alpha.

Like Theorem 1, the above result shows that the nonparametric based confidence regions are also asymptotically valid. However, unlike in the Gaussian prior setting, it is not necessary that the nonparametric empirical Bayes confidence regions have smaller Lebesgue measure than just using θ^0subscript^𝜃0\hat{\theta}_{0} as an asymptotic pivot. Despite this, the following hybrid approach ensures that the expected Lebesgue measure is no worse than the classical interval.

  1. 1.

    Estimate π^n​(⋅)subscript^𝜋𝑛⋅\hat{\pi}_{n}(\cdot) as above.

  2. 2.

    Simulate data according to the hierarchical model

    θ^|θconditional^𝜃𝜃\displaystyle\hat{\theta}|\theta ∼𝒩​(θ,σ^2/n0)similar-toabsent𝒩𝜃superscript^𝜎2subscript𝑛0\displaystyle\sim\mathcal{N}(\theta,\hat{\sigma}^{2}/n_{0})
    θ𝜃\displaystyle\theta ∼π^nsimilar-toabsentsubscript^𝜋𝑛\displaystyle\sim\hat{\pi}_{n}

    and compute the empirical Bayes confidence regions.

  3. 3.

    Calculate the expected Lebesgue measure using the results of (2).

    If the expected Lebesgue measure is less than 2​zα/2​σ^2/n02subscript𝑧𝛼2superscript^𝜎2subscript𝑛02z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\hat{\sigma}^{2}/n_{0}}, apply the proposed empirical Bayes confidence region to θ^0subscript^𝜃0\hat{\theta}_{0}.

    Else, construct the classical confidence interval using θ^0subscript^𝜃0\hat{\theta}_{0}.

We emphasize that this hybrid approach still maintains asymptotic coverage. Since the decision to use empirical Bayes confidence regions or classical confidence intervals only depends on data independent of θ^0subscript^𝜃0\hat{\theta}_{0} and θ0subscript𝜃0\theta_{0}, this does not affect the coverage guaranteed by Theorem 2.

6 Simulations

In this section, we evaluate the empirical performance of our proposed procedure. As the prototypical examples of asymptotically linear estimators, we use the least-squares estimator and the debiased lasso (cf. ? (?), ? (?), and ? (?)) from Example 3. In particular, for a linear model

𝒚k=𝐗k​𝜷k+𝜺k,subscript𝒚𝑘subscript𝐗𝑘subscript𝜷𝑘subscript𝜺𝑘\displaystyle\boldsymbol{y}_{k}=\mathbf{X}_{k}\boldsymbol{\beta}_{k}+\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{k},

where 𝒚k,𝜺k∈ℝnksubscript𝒚𝑘subscript𝜺𝑘superscriptℝsubscript𝑛𝑘\boldsymbol{y}_{k},\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{k}\in\mathbb{R}^{n_{k}}, 𝐗∈ℝnk×p𝐗superscriptℝsubscript𝑛𝑘𝑝\mathbf{X}\in\mathbb{R}^{n_{k}\times p}, and 𝜷k∈ℝpsubscript𝜷𝑘superscriptℝ𝑝\boldsymbol{\beta}_{k}\in\mathbb{R}^{p}, we consider θk=𝜷k,1subscript𝜃𝑘subscript𝜷𝑘1{\theta}_{k}=\boldsymbol{\beta}_{k,1}, the first entry of 𝜷ksubscript𝜷𝑘\boldsymbol{\beta}_{k}. Regarding the simulation settings, we vary K∈{20,50,100}𝐾2050100K\in\{20,50,100\} and set nk=100subscript𝑛𝑘100n_{k}=100 for k=1,…,K𝑘1…𝐾k=1,\dots,K. We generate 𝜺k∼𝒩nk​(𝟎nk,𝐈nk)similar-tosubscript𝜺𝑘subscript𝒩subscript𝑛𝑘subscript0subscript𝑛𝑘subscript𝐈subscript𝑛𝑘\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{k}\sim\mathcal{N}_{n_{k}}(\mathbf{0}_{n_{k}},\mathbf{I}_{n_{k}}). For the prior distribution, we consider π(⋅)=φ(⋅|1,σπ2)\pi(\cdot)=\varphi(\cdot|1,\sigma_{\pi}^{2}) and π(⋅)=0.5φ(⋅|1,σπ2)+0.5φ(⋅|−1,σπ2)\pi(\cdot)=0.5\varphi(\cdot|1,\sigma_{\pi}^{2})+0.5\varphi(\cdot|-1,\sigma_{\pi}^{2}) for σπ2∈{0.1,1}superscriptsubscript𝜎𝜋20.11\sigma_{\pi}^{2}\in\{0.1,1\}.

In the low-dimensional case, we let p=5𝑝5p=5 and set n0∈{0,20}subscript𝑛0020n_{0}\in\{0,20\}. Since the least-squares estimator is, in fact, linear with variance σ2=1/nksuperscript𝜎21subscript𝑛𝑘\sigma^{2}=1/n_{k}, we estimate the variance with the usual mean squared error estimator. In the high-dimensional case, we let p=500𝑝500p=500, sβ=‖𝜷‖0=3subscript𝑠𝛽subscriptnorm𝜷03s_{\beta}=\|\boldsymbol{\beta}\|_{0}=3, and n0={0,100}subscript𝑛00100n_{0}=\{0,100\}. Then, we compute the debiased lasso estimator using the hdi package in R.

To evaluate the performance of the empirical Bayes estimators, we apply the Gaussian empirical Bayes estimator from Theorem 1 (denoted EB-pa, where the “pa” stands for parametric), the nonparametric empirical Bayes estimator using kernel density (denoted EB-kd), and the nonparametric empirical Bayes estimator using deconvolution (denoted EB-dc). As a comparison, we include the oracle Bayes estimator that has access to the true distribution π​(⋅)𝜋⋅\pi(\cdot) (denoted OB) and the classical confidence interval using the asymptotic Gaussian approximation (denoted CL). The three confidence regions are evaluated on the basis of coverage at a nominal level of 95%percent9595\% and Lebesgue measure.

The results are presented in Tables 1 – 4. In general, we notice that kernel density outperforms deconvolution when the variance of the prior distribution π​(⋅)𝜋⋅\pi(\cdot) is small and deconvolution outperforms kernel density when the prior is more dispersed; this is consistent with the results of Section 5.2. Moreover, as the number of related populations increases, the performance of the empirical Bayes estimator also improves. In general, the Lebesgue measure of the empirical Bayes confidence intervals is smaller than that of the classical confidence intervals while maintaining comparable coverage, corroborating our theoretical results.

7 Application to the Trends in International Mathematics and Sciences Study (TIMSS)

The Trends in International Mathematics and Sciences Study is an international study conducted every four years to measure fourth and eighth grade achievement in mathematics and sciences. Polities sample representative schools, and the students within the schools take standardized examinations in mathematics and science. The data is freely available at https://timssandpirls.bc.edu/; in addition to an overall measure of achievement for each school, we also have access to various school background covariates. These include, for example, the total enrollment of the school, the amount of students from economically disadvantaged backgrounds, amongst others. For our analysis, we focus only on fourth grade achievement in mathematics in 2015. For a more detailed description of the methodology of TIMSS and a general overview, we refer the interested reader to ? (?) and ? (?) respectively.

We are interested in seeing which school background covariates significantly influence student achievement (i) in the United States by leveraging the observations in other polities and (ii) in a new, unobserved polity. In the context of our first problem, we view polities as the various populations P0,P1,…,PKsubscript𝑃0subscript𝑃1…subscript𝑃𝐾P_{0},P_{1},\dots,P_{K}, with P0subscript𝑃0P_{0} denoting the United States. Then, within each polity, the schools comprise our observational units. Since some covariates have very low variability within a polity, such as the amount of digital magazines with different titles, we omit those covariates; this leaves us with p=90𝑝90p=90 distinct covariates. Moreover, we only consider polities with nk≥50subscript𝑛𝑘50n_{k}\geq 50 schools for a total of K=34𝐾34K=34. Regarding the model, we assume a high-dimensional linear model and apply the debiased lasso. For the unobserved polity, the formulation is nearly identical, except we have K=35𝐾35K=35 distinct polities from which we estimate the distribution.

We only apply deconvolution since we believe the variation amongst polities to be of larger order than the noise in estimating the parameters due to the relatively small sample size. Moreover, due to geographic and economic factors, we do not believe that the polities are necessarily Gaussian distributed. We consider each variable marginally, without correcting for multiple testing. All results are presented at the α=0.05𝛼0.05\alpha=0.05 level.
